title: Lube: cass-lube-scout prompt should skip rebuild since mirror-sync cron keeps index fresh
description:
CASS evidence: 283 hits for 'cass index stale run rebuild', 477 for 'sbp cass rebuild'. The cass-lube-scout.sh prompt tells agents to 'run sbp cass rebuild and wait for it to finish' when the index is stale. But the cass-mirror-sync.sh cron runs every 30 minutes and keeps the index fresh. A full rebuild (rclone sync + projection + reindex) takes 30-40 minutes and blocks the scout for its entire runtime.

Observed directly in this session: sbp cass status was stale, but the index had 232K matches and was fully searchable. The mirror-sync cron had already refreshed it. Running a full rebuild was unnecessary — the scout just needed the index to be queryable, which it was.

No-ragrets success contract:
- Outcome: cass-lube-scout.sh prompt checks status, proceeds with mining if the index is queryable (health OK or degraded with matches), and only blocks on rebuild if the index is completely empty/broken.
- Evidence: this session wasted minutes on rebuild when the index was already usable. 283 sessions hit the same rebuild ceremony.
- Failure avoided: scout sessions burning 30+ minutes on unnecessary rebuilds when the cron already maintains freshness.
- Acceptance: (1) scout checks sbp cass status, (2) proceeds if probe shows matches, (3) only runs rebuild if zero matches or error, (4) cron keeps index fresh between scouts.
close_reason: Fix applied: updated cass-lube-scout.sh prompt to check status and proceed if probe has matches, only rebuild on zero matches/error. Uses --skip-archive-upload to avoid unnecessary R2 upload step.

title: Durable-state backup & restore drill
description:
EPIC: Give the durable state a real continuity story: first-class backup tooling for .skillbox-state plus a restore drill that converts the stewardship report's standing "not_assessed" verdict on backup/restore into dated evidence.

WHY: skillbox's core thesis is durable state (Design Stance #2) — agent homes, client overlays, logs, monoserver repos all live under SKILLBOX_STATE_ROOT — yet the project ships ZERO backup tooling and stewardship-report explicitly marks backup/restore drills as not_assessed (README: "important hardening domains that are not yet proven"). A box that remembers everything until the first disk failure remembers nothing.

SCOPE BOUNDARY: operator-grade local backup + verified restore. NOT in scope: offsite replication, scheduling/retention policy automation, encryption-at-rest (the state root may contain secrets — backups inherit that posture and the docs say so loudly; offsite/encryption are follow-up decisions once the primitive exists).

EXIT: one command produces a verifiable backup; one command proves a backup restores; stewardship cites the latest drill with a date instead of not_assessed.

title: state-backup create/list/verify with checksummed manifest
description:
OBJECTIVE: Implement `manage.py state-backup create|list|verify` — tar.gz snapshots of SKILLBOX_STATE_ROOT with a checksummed manifest — as a registered, structured-output command.

BACKGROUND: .skillbox-state holds everything the box's durability promise covers: clients/, home/.claude + .codex (agent memory, skills, sessions), logs/, monoserver/ (potentially large repo checkouts). Today the operator's only option is ad-hoc tar — no manifest, no verification, no exclusion intelligence, no integration with doctor/stewardship.

REASONING: backup needs to be one boring command or it will not happen; making it a registry command gives it MCP visibility, capabilities listing, and the standard payload contract for free.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) create: stream a tar.gz of the state root to ${SKILLBOX_BACKUP_ROOT:-<state-root-sibling>/skillbox-backups}/skillbox-state-<utc-stamp>.tar.gz — destination must be OUTSIDE the state root (no recursive backups) and validated against being inside it; write alongside it a manifest json {created_at, source_root, file_count, total_bytes, sha256 (of the tarball), tool_version, excludes_applied, top_level_entries:[{path, bytes}]}. (2) Default excludes (overridable --include-all): caches and reproducibles — monoserver/ (repos re-clone; INCLUDE by flag --with-monoserver), logs/ older than N days? NO — keep logs, they are the box's memory; exclude only known cache dirs (pruned-skill-repo-extras-*, rch-adapter caches, __pycache__) — the exclude table is explicit in code with a comment per entry. (3) verify: recompute tarball sha256 against manifest + structural spot-check (tar -t parses, manifest top_level_entries present). (4) list: table of backups with age/size/verified-status. (5) Registry: tier 2, side_effect local_write, risk low; MCP mirror skillbox_state_backup gated behind the dry-run-first discipline? create is additive (never overwrites) — plain call acceptable, document why. (6) Secrets: the tarball contains agent homes (tokens in ~/.claude etc.) — manifest and docs state this in bold; backups land mode 0600. (7) Pulse/doctor advisory: doctor warns (advisory, not failing) when newest backup age > SKILLBOX_BACKUP_MAX_AGE_DAYS (default off / opt-in env).

FILES: runtime_manager (new module state_backup.py + cli + registry entries), .env.example (SKILLBOX_BACKUP_ROOT, MAX_AGE opt-in), mcp_server.py mirror, tests/test_state_backup.py (new), docs/operations page section.

RISKS: large state roots (stream, never load into memory; report progress per top-level dir in text mode); backing up WHILE pulse writes state (acceptable for v1 — note "consistent-enough" semantics in docs; a quiesce flag stopping pulse around the tar is a noted follow-up, not built now); disk exhaustion mid-write (pre-check free space >= estimated size via du of source, abort early with PRESSURE-style error — reuse pressure-report helpers if convenient).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) create on a fixture state root produces tarball+manifest, sha256 verifies, excludes honored, mode 0600; (2) verify detects a flipped byte; (3) list renders ages/sizes; (4) destination-inside-source rejected; (5) free-space pre-check aborts cleanly; (6) registered + visible in capabilities + API reference regenerates.

TESTING: fixture-tree round-trips; corruption detection; exclusion table tests; space-check with mocked statvfs; registry/golden updates.

OBSERVABILITY: structured payload per the house style {ok, steps, artifact_path, manifest}; runtime.log line on create.

MAINTAINER NOTES: Restore intentionally lives in the DRILL bead (restore-to-temp first, in-place restore is a deliberate second step there) — keep create/verify/list shippable without it.
close_reason: done

title: state-backup drill + guarded restore + stewardship evidence integration
description:
OBJECTIVE: Implement `state-backup drill` (restore-to-temp + structural validation) and an in-place `state-backup restore` with heavy guardrails, then wire drill evidence into stewardship-report so backup/restore flips from not_assessed to dated assessment.

BACKGROUND: a backup nobody has restored is a hope, not a backup. stewardship-report (README: marks "backup/restore drills and cost review as not_assessed") already models exactly this gap — the drill closes the loop: prove restorability mechanically, on a schedule the operator controls, with evidence the reporting surface consumes.

REASONING: the drill-to-temp design makes proving restore SAFE (no production state touched), so it can run casually — which is the only way it runs at all.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) drill: pick newest (or --backup <path>) verified backup -> extract to a temp dir -> structural validation: expected top-level entries present (vs manifest), key invariants hold (clients/*/overlay.yaml parse as YAML, home/.claude exists, skill lockfiles parse, no path escapes during extraction — use tarfile filter='data' (py3.12+) / equivalent safe-extract for older), file count within tolerance of manifest -> write drill evidence json under the state root ({ts, backup_path, sha256, checks:[...], ok}) -> clean up temp (keep on --keep for inspection). (2) restore (in-place): REQUIRES --i-understand-data-loss flag + an automatic safety backup of current state FIRST (reuse create) + refuses while pulse is running (pid check) + refuses on sha256 mismatch; restores to a staging sibling then swaps (atomic-ish via rename dance documented honestly); this is the one command in the plan that can destroy state — its test coverage and its guardrails are the bulk of the work. (3) stewardship-report: read latest drill evidence; backup/restore domain shows {last_drill, age_days, ok} and reverts to not_assessed when age > 30d (configurable) — the audit surface drives the habit. (4) Registry entries; MCP mirrors restore NOT exposed over MCP (operator-terminal-only, documented why: destructive + interactive confidence required).

FILES: runtime_manager/state_backup.py (extend), stewardship report module, cli/registry, tests, docs.

RISKS: unsafe tar extraction (use the stdlib data filter; explicit test with a malicious member fixture); restore swap failing midway (the pre-restore safety backup is the recovery; the swap procedure logs each step so a manual unwind is scriptable); drill temp space (same free-space pre-check as create).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) drill on a healthy backup passes all checks and writes evidence; (2) drill on a corrupted backup fails naming the failed check; (3) malicious-member tarball is neutralized (test); (4) restore refuses without the flag, with pulse running, and on checksum mismatch; (5) restore round-trip on a fixture (create -> mutate -> restore -> state equals original) passes; (6) stewardship shows the dated drill and decays to not_assessed past the window.

TESTING: as above plus stewardship integration test with planted evidence files of varying ages.

OBSERVABILITY: drill evidence json is the artifact; runtime.log lines for drill/restore start+outcome; restore prints a step-by-step transcript.

MAINTAINER NOTES: The not_assessed decay is the feature that keeps this honest forever — resist making evidence permanent. If the operator stops drilling, the report should start nagging again.

title: Fix runtime defects: zombie liveness, cwd-inference re-sort, durable session ordering
description:
Random-fix pass found and fixed three real defects plus stale tests after the recent sbp/box work.

1. process_is_running treated zombie (defunct) processes as alive: os.kill(pid,0) succeeds for a zombie, so stop_process reported 'stuck' and the auto-heal restart path refused to restart an unhealthy service whose process had become a child zombie. Fixed by adding zombie detection via /proc/<pid>/stat state 'Z' (Linux; safe fallback elsewhere). File: .env-manager/runtime_manager/runtime_ops.py.

2. _runtime_client_matches_for_cwd destructively re-sorted matched_skill_clients output by (-match_len, id), discarding the upstream default_cwd tiebreak. A client whose default_cwd is exactly the cwd could lose to an alphabetically earlier client sharing only a cwd_match prefix. Fixed with a stable sort on match length only. File: .env-manager/runtime_manager/cli.py.

3. read_durable_session_events returned events in non-deterministic filesystem glob order instead of chronological order. Fixed by sorting by (ts, source, line_number, subject), matching event_feed_payload. File: .env-manager/runtime_manager/shared.py.

Also repaired stale tests after feat commit 37ee325 (larger box profile support): box swimmers-gate fixture missing profile.ssh_user; box_lifecycle monoserver host-root assertion updated from /monoserver to /repos to match shipped .env.example + docker-compose contract.

Validation: full suite green (1148 tests OK, was failures=4 errors=2); CI render check (scripts/04-reconcile.py render) exits 0. New zombie unit tests fail before / pass after.
close_reason: Fixed and validated; full unittest suite green (1148 OK), CI render exit 0, new zombie tests fail-before/pass-after. Committed in random-fix pass.

title: test_box_lifecycle remote contract test is env-dependent: reads box .env SKILLBOX_MONOSERVER_HOST_ROOT
description: Evidence (2026-06-11): tests/test_box_lifecycle.py::test_remote_box_contract_payload_uses_state_root_and_derived_swimmers_token fails on this box at line 528 (expected SKILLBOX_MONOSERVER_HOST_ROOT=/srv/skillbox/repos, got /srv/skillbox/monoserver) both with and without local changes (verified on a clean HEAD worktree). Box .env sets SKILLBOX_MONOSERVER_HOST_ROOT=/srv/repos, so the test inherits host-local config instead of a pinned fixture. Fix: isolate the test from the local .env/environment (patch env or pass explicit overrides). Full-suite status otherwise: 1254 tests, 1 failure (this one), 3 skipped. Pre-existing environmental blocker made explicit for skillbox-agent-ops-integration-hardening-5krw; found during skillbox-agent-ops-sbp-visibility-hardening-lht.

title: Define BoxNetworkPosture policy contract
description: Define a compact BoxNetworkPosture contract that future boxes can carry in inventory/profile/state: expected mode such as tailnet_only, allowed public ingress, cloud firewall identity, host firewall expectations, allowed tailnet app ports, app exposure policy, and proof requirements. Keep it provider-adapter friendly but implementable for DigitalOcean first. Model route and authority: Codex gpt-5.5 owns orchestration, system design, implementation, review, commit acceptance, and final say. Claude Opus is only for UX/docs visual review if a docs surface needs design judgment. Grok/Composer 2.5 may run bounded probes only when the validation commands and stronger-model review are explicit. Failure avoided: status cannot confuse observed reachability with desired policy.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: docs/design or code contract names fields, defaults, migration behavior for old inventory entries, and policy-failure semantics. It explicitly distinguishes public interface, Tailnet interface, loopback, and wildcard binds. Validation: focused unit tests or schema tests prove old inventory remains readable and new policy defaults to tailnet_only for managed boxes.
notes: No-ragrets contract. Outcome: every box has an explicit desired network posture instead of inferred safety. Evidence: fields, defaults, migration behavior, provider scope, policy-failure semantics, and tests for old and new inventory. Failure avoided: status reports facts but cannot say whether public, Tailnet, loopback, or wildcard binds violate policy.
close_reason: Contract implemented: network_posture + cloud_firewall_id fields on Box, resolve_network_posture migration, policy functions, evaluate_posture_violations evaluator, 24 tests covering old inventory compat, defaults, policy rules, and inventory roundtrip.

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text:
## BoxNetworkPosture Contract — Implementation Evidence

### Fields Added to Box Dataclass (scripts/box.py)

- `network_posture: str = ""` — desired posture. Valid values: `tailnet_only`, `public`, `unmanaged`. Empty string is the migration default for old inventory.
- `cloud_firewall_id: str | None = None` — optional reference to a cloud provider firewall (e.g. DigitalOcean firewall ID).

### Posture Constants

- `POSTURE_TAILNET_ONLY = "tailnet_only"` — SSH restricted to Tailnet CIDR, cloud firewall required, wildcard-direct app binds forbidden
- `POSTURE_PUBLIC = "public"` — public SSH allowed, no cloud firewall required, all app binds allowed
- `POSTURE_UNMANAGED = "unmanaged"` — no posture enforcement (for external/registered boxes)

### Migration Behavior

- `resolve_network_posture(box)` resolves empty/missing field to safe defaults:
  - Managed boxes (management_mode="managed") → tailnet_only
  - External boxes (management_mode="external") → unmanaged
- Old inventory without the field loads cleanly via existing `Box(**{k: v for ...})` pattern — unknown keys are filtered, new keys get dataclass defaults

### Policy Functions

- `posture_allows_public_ssh(posture)` — True only for "public"
- `posture_requires_cloud_firewall(posture)` — True only for "tailnet_only"
- `posture_requires_host_ssh_lockdown(posture)` — True only for "tailnet_only"
- `posture_allows_exposure(posture, exposure)` — False for wildcard-direct on tailnet_only, True for everything else

### Exposure Classification (Distinguished Bind Types)

- `wildcard-direct` — app binds 0.0.0.0, reachable on all interfaces (VIOLATION under tailnet_only)
- `tailnet-direct` — app binds Tailnet IP directly (allowed under tailnet_only)
- `ingress-routed` — app accessible via ingress proxy on Tailnet (allowed under tailnet_only)
- `loopback-only` — app binds localhost/127.x only (allowed under tailnet_only, not Tailnet-reachable)

### Policy-Failure Semantics

`evaluate_posture_violations(box, network_checks=..., app_exposures=...)` returns a list of violation dicts:
- `public_ssh_reachable` (severity: error) — SSH reachable on public IP under tailnet_only posture
- `cloud_firewall_missing` (severity: warning) — no cloud firewall ID under tailnet_only posture
- `app_exposure_violation` (severity: error) — wildcard-direct app bind under tailnet_only posture

Unmanaged boxes return no violations (no enforcement).

### Tests (24 passing)

- Old inventory loads without posture field, Box.network_posture defaults to ""
- Old managed box resolves to tailnet_only, old external resolves to unmanaged
- Explicit posture values are respected
- New managed Box defaults to tailnet_only
- All posture policy functions tested for each posture value
- Violation evaluator tested for SSH, cloud firewall, and app exposure scenarios
- Posture roundtrips through inventory save/load

title: Add lockdown stage to box up lifecycle
description: Insert a first-class lockdown stage into box up: create -> storage -> bootstrap -> ssh-ready -> enroll -> lockdown -> deploy -> contract/launch/verify. After lockdown, deploy and future status should prefer Tailnet targets and treat public SSH as policy drift, not a convenience fallback. Model route and authority: Codex gpt-5.5 owns orchestration, system design, implementation, review, commit acceptance, and final say. Claude Opus is only for UX/docs visual review if a docs surface needs design judgment. Grok/Composer 2.5 may run bounded probes only when the validation commands and stronger-model review are explicit. Stop condition: bootstrap failure must still be recoverable with clear next_actions.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: dry-run and real JSON step payloads include lockdown; resume behavior knows which states can safely repeat lockdown; next_actions never tell a ready tailnet-only box to use public SSH except for explicit recovery. Validation: tests cover new stage ordering, failure-state transitions, resume from ssh-ready/enrolling/deploying, and text/json output.
notes: No-ragrets contract. Outcome: box up has an explicit lockdown stage between enrollment and normal deploy/status operations. Evidence: dry-run and real JSON step payloads, resumable state transitions, safe next_actions, and tests for ordering/failure/resume. Failure avoided: ready boxes keep treating public SSH as the normal path after Tailscale exists.
close_reason: Added lockdown stage to box state machine between enrolling and deploying, with host firewall verification and cloud firewall lifecycle. All tests pass (1 pre-existing failure unrelated).

title: Make br auto-inject read-only flags in non-TTY (agent) contexts
description:
Friction: br auto-import/daemon flakiness requires agents to pass --allow-stale --no-auto-flush --no-auto-import on every invocation. In session f2053074 (lines 77-132), the agent discovered br blocked --json was flaky without these three flags. Every subsequent br call (17 total with --db) had to carry all three flags. The beads-bv skill warns about this, but agents still rediscover it each session.

Cass evidence: session f2053074 lines L77-L132 — 17 br calls each with 3 safety flags. The beads-bv skill documents the workaround at SKILL.md line ~160 but agents skip it.

Lube fix (smallest durable unblocker): Default br to read-only behavior (--allow-stale --no-auto-flush --no-auto-import) when stdout is not a TTY (agent context). This matches the actual safe behavior agents need without requiring explicit flags on every call.

No-ragrets contract:
- Outcome: agents can use bare br commands without the 3-flag tax in non-TTY contexts.
- Evidence: 17 calls with redundant flags in one session, likely recurs in every cross-repo triage.
- Failure avoided: agent omits a flag, gets a flaky result, has to rediscover the workaround.
- Acceptance: (1) br commands in non-TTY contexts default to read-only mode, (2) br in TTY still has current behavior, (3) --no-auto-flush etc. still work explicitly.
notes: BLOCKER: requires changes to br binary (beads_rust), not skillbox. br must detect non-TTY and auto-inject --allow-stale --no-auto-flush --no-auto-import.

comments.author: skillbox

title: Make br --db accept a directory and auto-discover .db file
description:
Friction: br --db expects a .db file path, not a directory. Agents naturally try --db .beads/ (the conventional path they encounter), get 'DATABASE_ERROR: I/O error: Is a directory (os error 21)', then have to glob *.db to find the actual filename. 5+ retries observed in session f2053074 lines 63-127.

Cass evidence: session f2053074 lines L63, L67, L74, L77, L81 — five successive inventory attempts before settling on ls .beads/*.db workaround.

Lube fix (smallest durable unblocker): When --db receives a directory path, auto-discover the first .db file inside it. If multiple .db files exist, error with a list of candidates.

No-ragrets contract:
- Outcome: br --db .beads/ just works — the natural agent path succeeds on first try.
- Evidence: 5+ retries in one session, likely recurs in every cross-repo bead triage.
- Failure avoided: agent wastes 3-5 tool calls discovering the .db filename inside a .beads/ directory.
- Acceptance: (1) br --db .beads/ discovers and uses .beads/*.db, (2) br --db .beads/ with multiple .db files errors with candidates list, (3) br --db path/to/beads.db still works.
notes: BLOCKER: requires changes to br binary (beads_rust). br --db must resolve directories to the .db file inside them.

title: Make br flush report comment counts instead of misleading Nothing to export
description:
Friction: After agents add comments to beads, br flush reports 'Nothing to export (no dirty issues).' This is misleading — comments were written but issue records didn't change. Agents get confused and spend tokens justifying why flush says nothing happened. 8 instances of this confusion in session f2053074 lines 165-179, across 8 different subagents who independently had to explain the output.

Cass evidence: session f2053074 lines L165, L166, L171, L172, L173, L178, L179 — eight subagents each independently confused by the misleading output.

Lube fix (smallest durable unblocker): Change br flush to report '0 issues changed, N comments persisted' when comments were written but no issue records changed. The current 'Nothing to export' message is technically correct but operationally misleading.

No-ragrets contract:
- Outcome: br flush output accurately reflects what was persisted, including comments.
- Evidence: 8 agent confusion instances in one session, all spending tokens rationalizing the output.
- Failure avoided: agent sees 'Nothing to export', assumes comments weren't saved, potentially re-adds them or reports failure.
- Acceptance: (1) br flush after comment-only changes reports comment count, (2) br flush with no changes at all still says nothing to export, (3) br flush with issue changes reports both counts.
notes: BLOCKER: requires changes to br binary (beads_rust). flush must report comment mutations, not just issue-field mutations.

title: CASS rebuild policy: local-machine 5am PST schedule, no inline agent rebuilds
description:
User decision on 2026-06-20: do not pursue inline CASS auto-rebuild inside agent search calls. sbp cass appears to run against local/operator-machine state, so rebuild ownership and timing must be coordinated with the operator local machine.

No-ragrets success contract:
- Outcome: CASS rebuilds run off-hours on the operator/local machine every morning at 5am Pacific time, using the canonical sbp/cass command for that machine.
- Evidence: a scheduler entry or equivalent local-machine automation exists; rebuild logs prove daily completion; sbp cass status is fresh after the scheduled run; agents no longer trigger rebuilds during active work.
- Failure avoided: an agent blocks or mutates local CASS state mid-task, collides with operator-machine ownership, or quietly changes evidence freshness while another agent is relying on it.

Blocked until: the operator/local-machine scheduling target, timezone handling, and canonical rebuild command are confirmed. Do not implement a repo-local cron/timer blindly from the devbox.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Document the canonical rebuild command and machine that owns it.
2. Install or document the 5am Pacific schedule with logs and failure notification behavior.
3. Add guidance that agents must not run CASS rebuilds during active task work without explicit operator approval.
4. Verify with sbp cass status/log evidence after the first scheduled run.

Validation: sbp cass status --json; inspect scheduler/log evidence on the local machine; br show this bead records the final owner and command before closure.
notes: BLOCKER: requires operator-machine cron/scheduler configuration for local CASS rebuild scheduling. Not a skillbox code change; blocked on local-machine policy implementation.

comments.author: rob-ask-cascade
comments.text: OPERATOR DECISION 2026-06-21 (Rob): CASS rebuilds run 5am Pacific DAILY on the operator's LOCAL machine via the canonical sbp/cass command. Agents must NEVER inline-rebuild during search; fall back to recent logs if stale. Remaining work = operator-local cron setup (not runnable from the devbox).

title: No-ragrets lube: Cass rebuild freshness UX and health semantics
description:
No-ragrets success contract for the Cass rebuild lube pass.

Outcome: future agents can tell whether central Cass search is usable, which freshness phase failed, and what can safely continue without reconstructing this incident from chat.
Evidence: br child tasks define concrete validation commands for sbp cass status/search/rebuild dry-run, archive upload phase reporting, and cron executable checks; implementation closes each child only with before/after command output.
Failure avoided: transient archive upload failures, slow cass status, or cron permission drift no longer make agents think the whole Cass DB is missing or unusable.

Scope: skillbox-config/sbp Cass wrapper, devbox mirror-sync cron/install validation, and docs/runbook wording surfaced through Skillbox. Do not rotate secrets, delete raw archive data, or run destructive box lifecycle commands.
Model route and authority: Codex gpt-5.5 orchestrates/designs/reviews; bounded task runners may collect logs or run dry-run probes; Codex has final acceptance authority.
Stop condition: all child tasks either closed with validation evidence or explicitly blocked with the missing prerequisite named.
close_reason: Epic complete: all four child tasks closed with validation evidence. Implemented Cass rebuild phase reporting, upload-failure/search-availability semantics, fast status fallback, mirror BUSY non-success handling, and read-only doctor checks for the devbox mirror sync executable path. Live proof: sbp cass search returns status=ok total_matches=151433; devbox sbp cass doctor --json returns all checks ok.

title: Cass rebuild reports phase, machine, and usability during archive catch-up
description: Observed friction: sbp cass rebuild reads like a derived-index rebuild, but actually runs laptop upload, devbox upload, mirror sync, projection, and index. Durable unblocker: user-facing JSON and docs name the active phase and preserve whether existing central search remains usable. Write scope: sbp cass wrapper and focused tests/docs only.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: sbp cass rebuild --dry-run --json exposes ordered phases for operator-laptop upload, portfolio-devbox upload, mirror sync/projection/index; failure payloads include failed phase, machine, archive_upload status, and central search usability guidance; when mirror sync/index returns BUSY after archive upload, rebuild retries within the timeout and otherwise reports mirror_busy/non-success instead of ok while preserving central search usability guidance; validation includes a focused unit/fixture test or equivalent mocked command proof plus python3 -m py_compile for touched scripts.
close_reason: Implemented Cass rebuild phase/usability semantics in skillbox-config sbp_cass.py: ordered phases for operator-laptop upload, portfolio-devbox upload, mirror sync, source projection, and cass index; central_search_usability guidance; mirror BUSY retry now returns mirror_busy exit 75 instead of ok. Validation: python3 -m unittest tests.test_sbp_cass; python3 -m py_compile scripts/sbp_cass.py scripts/agent_memory_raw_jsonl_uploader.py; local and devbox dry-run skip-upload probes showed ordered phases and proof command.

title: Cass upload failures do not obscure existing central search availability
description: Observed friction: archive_upload_error fatally gated rebuild and made a usable existing Cass DB look broken. Durable unblocker: distinguish freshness catch-up failure from search/index availability, and point future agents at safe next commands. Write scope: wrapper status/rebuild output and runbook wording; no secret or R2 policy changes.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: simulated uploader failure returns archive_upload_error with exact leg, receipt/log path when available, and message that existing sbp cass search may still be usable; sbp cass search '*' --json --fields minimal --limit 1 remains the recommended proof; validation includes a mocked uploader-failure test or reproducible dry-run fixture.
close_reason: Implemented archive_upload_error payload semantics: failed_phase carries exact leg/machine/receipt paths when available, message says existing central search may still be usable, and central_search_usability.proof_command points to sbp cass search '*' --json --fields minimal --limit 1. Validation: mocked uploader-failure unittest plus live search proof status=ok total_matches=151433.

title: Cass status uses fast health plus search probe instead of slow cass status as readiness
description: Observed friction: cass status --json timed out while cass health and search worked, producing misleading degraded status. Durable unblocker: front-door status treats health+search as operator readiness and records slow status as detail. Write scope: sbp_cass.py status path and tests.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: when remote cass status times out but cass health exits 0 and search returns total_matches > 0, sbp cass status --json reports healthy/ok with a status_note and preserves the timeout exit code; when health fails but search works, status is degraded with explicit reason; validation covers both branches.
close_reason: Implemented fast readiness fallback: slow cass status exit is preserved, health+search success reports ok with status_note, and health failure with search success reports degraded with explicit reason. Validation: focused unittest covers both branches. Live probe currently reports degraded because cass health says index stale, while search remains usable with total_matches=151433.

title: Doctor catches non-executable devbox cron and symlink targets for Cass mirror sync
description: Observed friction: cass-mirror-sync.sh was symlinked into ~/.local/bin but the target was not executable, so cron failed with Permission denied. Durable unblocker: a doctor/check validates executable bits for installed cron targets before cron silently rots. Write scope: Skillbox config doctor/cron validation and focused tests.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: a read-only doctor/check reports failure when /home/skillbox/.local/bin/cass-mirror-sync.sh or its symlink target is not executable; fix guidance names chmod +x on the source-controlled script; validation includes a local fixture test and a safe remote read-only probe command in notes.
close_reason: Implemented read-only sbp cass doctor for Cass mirror sync executable checks. It validates /home/skillbox/.local/bin/cass-mirror-sync.sh, resolved target /srv/skillbox/repos/skillbox-config/scripts/cass-mirror-sync.sh, and chmod +x fix guidance. Validation: local temp-symlink fixture unittest catches non-executable target; live devbox sbp cass doctor --json returned all checks ok.

title: Make sbp cass rebuild non-fatal on archive upload failure
description:
Friction: sbp cass rebuild gates the entire index rebuild on a prerequisite archive upload to R2 (sbp_cass.py lines 531-541). If the upload fails (network timeout, stale creds, SCP failure), rebuild returns early with exit 2 — the mirror-sync and reindex on lines 550-575 never execute. Agents get a false failure and the index stays stale.

Cass evidence: session d1664698 line 142-155 (prior rebuild), current session 2026-06-10 (archive_upload_error hit on this box).

Lube fix (smallest durable unblocker): Demote upload failure from fatal gate to warning. Record it in the payload as archive_upload_warning. Proceed with existing mirror data. Add --strict flag to preserve fatal behavior for CI.

No-ragrets contract:
- Outcome: sbp cass rebuild is self-healing — a flaky upload does not leave the index degraded.
- Evidence: two sessions where archive_upload_error blocked a rebuild that had viable local mirror data.
- Failure avoided: operator or agent runs sbp cass rebuild, gets exit 2, leaves index degraded, wastes a session re-diagnosing.
- Acceptance: (1) rebuild completes index even when upload fails, (2) payload contains archive_upload_warning, (3) --strict restores fatal, (4) existing tests pass.
close_reason: Demoted archive upload from fatal gate to warning; added --strict flag. Rebuild now continues with existing mirror data on upload failure. 4 tests verify non-fatal behavior, warning payload, and strict mode.

title: CASS immediate evidence fallback: recent logs instead of rebuild during active work
description:
Future behavior: when CASS is stale, degraded, or unavailable during an active agent task, agents should not rebuild the CASS index. They should use the recent-log/local transcript fallback path that skill-issue already documents for transcript evidence, then record that the evidence mode was degraded.

No-ragrets success contract:
- Outcome: skill-issue/CASS-facing guidance gives agents an exact fallback command/path for immediate evidence needs when CASS freshness is insufficient.
- Evidence: future agents encountering stale CASS use recent logs/local transcript scanning and report degraded evidence mode, rather than running sbp cass rebuild mid-task.
- Failure avoided: agents stall on index rebuilds, mutate local-machine search state, or skip evidence gathering entirely because CASS is stale.

Implementation considerations:
- Audit skill-issue evidence backend docs and sbp cass error/next_actions surfaces for stale/degraded CASS behavior.
- Prefer adding explicit next_actions such as skill-issue local scanner / recent transcript log path over adding any rebuild command.
- If the fallback belongs in skill-issue SKILL.md, use no-ragrets + skill-issue validation before editing.
- If the fallback belongs in sbp cass output, keep stdout/stderr and JSON contracts compact and testable.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Stale/degraded CASS guidance names a recent-log/local transcript fallback and does not recommend rebuild during active work.
2. The fallback path is tested or validated with a representative stale-CASS fixture or documented degraded-mode smoke.
3. skill-issue validation runs if SKILL.md changes.
4. Agent-facing output names the evidence mode so future users can tell CASS was bypassed.

Validation candidates: skill-issue quick_validate.py if edited; sbp cass status/search degraded-mode smoke if the CLI surface changes; focused tests for any changed runtime/sbp code.
close_reason: Documented degraded CASS evidence mode and local transcript fallback in bundled skill-issue guidance and sbp capabilities/help; validated with degraded sbp cass status smoke and focused tests.

title: Make cass search error on missing query argument
description:
Friction: cass search invoked with no query token in 96% of invocations (396 of 414 observed). Agents paste the bare command template from SKILL.md without filling in the query. cass silently returns empty or full corpus instead of erroring, so the agent doesn't realize the mistake.

Cass evidence: prior deep-dive session 9c51acce — command-shape histogram across all projects. 396/414 cass search calls had no positional arg.

Lube fix (smallest durable unblocker): Two changes: (1) cass search with no argv prints a one-line "missing QUERY positional — example: cass search 'foo bar'" and exits non-zero. (2) cass SKILL.md: replace bare cass search examples with cass search "<intent>" and add "the query is required" callout.

No-ragrets contract:
- Outcome: agents get immediate feedback when they omit the query, instead of silently getting useless results.
- Evidence: 96% misuse rate across 414 invocations — the current silent behavior trains agents to believe cass search is broken.
- Failure avoided: agent invokes cass search, gets empty results, concludes cass is broken, falls back to raw grep (observed in 72 sessions).
- Acceptance: (1) cass search with no args exits non-zero with usage hint, (2) SKILL.md examples include query placeholder, (3) cass search "real query" still works.
notes: BLOCKER: requires changes to cass binary. cass search must error when invoked without a query argument instead of silently returning empty.

title: Add Conference1 tailnet Serve and heavy-build visibility to SBP
description:
Context:
Conference1 is now the preferred heavy local machine for Swimmers-style Rust builds/tests and has working Tailscale Serve mappings for tailnet-only dev surfaces. Current host facts from the setup run:
- Tailscale MagicDNS: conference1.tail4c481e.ts.net
- Tailscale IP: 100.123.217.11
- SSH automation path: worker@100.123.217.11, with WSL Ubuntu for build workloads
- Windows helper for true Tailscale Serve: C:\Users\worker\bin\tailnet-serve.ps1 list|expose|remove
- Active Serve mappings use MagicDNS host URLs and forward to 127.0.0.1 backends on ports 3000, 3001, 3170, 3210, and 8050
- Raw Tailscale-IP portproxy exists as fallback, but the durable/clean path should be Tailscale Serve and tailnet-only semantics

Problem:
This knowledge currently lives in operator chat and machine-local helpers. Skillbox/SBP should expose it as first-class environment metadata and commands so future agents do not rediscover the same host/port/Tailscale constraints or keep using the wrong machine for heavy Swimmers validation.

Requested work:
1. Add Conference1 to the appropriate Skillbox/SBP environment metadata so agents can discover it as a heavy-build/dev-surface target.
2. Add or extend an SBP/Skillbox command surface that can list the known Conference1 tailnet URLs and, where appropriate, call the Windows tailnet-serve helper over SSH to list/expose/remove ports.
3. Keep app startup in repo-local scripts or app package scripts; keep Tailscale Serve management in host/SBP infrastructure, not random npm scripts.
4. Capture the Swimmers remote Rust validation lane: SWIMMERS_REMOTE_RUST_HOST=conference1-ssh or worker@100.123.217.11 plus WSL Ubuntu and persistent Cargo cache /var/tmp/swimmers-remote-rust-cache.
5. Document the security rule clearly: use Tailscale Serve/MagicDNS for tailnet access, do not require 0.0.0.0 public binds, and do not use Funnel unless explicitly requested.

Acceptance criteria:
- From the d/Skillbox environment, an agent can run one documented command to see Conference1 endpoints, helper commands, and current Serve status.
- The command output distinguishes MagicDNS Serve URLs from raw-IP portproxy fallback URLs.
- The implementation has at least one focused test or fixture proving Conference1 metadata renders without leaking secrets.
- README/agent docs mention the operator workflow briefly enough that future agents can route Swimmers heavy validation to Conference1 without chat archaeology.
- Proof includes a tailnet-only posture check or documented manual check path.
notes: ROUTING: requires Conference1 machine access and private overlay configuration. Operator-specific infrastructure bead — needs private skillbox-config context.

title: Deep research (GTM): Which channels + content formats drive OSS dev-tool CLI adoption for single-operator infra projects?
description:
**Why this matters for skillbox (marketing/GTM):** skillbox is an OSS CLI/Docker project targeting independent developers and consultants who want a private Tailnet-first agent workstation. Unlike SaaS dev tools, its acquisition path is entirely community-driven — GitHub discovery, developer word-of-mouth, Show HN, and ecosystem cross-links. Understanding which specific distribution moves (awesome-lists, comparisons, HN posts, X threads, YouTube walkthroughs) actually drive sustained GitHub star growth and downstream forks/installs for comparable single-operator infra OSS projects would define whether to invest in content, community presence, or focused comparison pages.

**Round-1 deep-research already covers:** Tailscale-first private agent infra adoption vs managed runtimes (product/market landscape).

---

## Deep research prompt (marketing / GTM / SEO / content)

**Role + mission:** You are an OSS developer-tool growth researcher. Your sole task is to identify which specific distribution channels, content formats, and community engagement tactics drive sustained GitHub star growth and downstream adoption for single-operator infrastructure CLI tools (not SaaS, not team dev-platforms) in 2026. You do NOT analyze product-market fit, compare architectures, or produce generic marketing advice.

**Context:**

skillbox is an open-source CLI + Docker-based personal agent workstation for independent developers running coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor) on a private Tailscale-connected machine. It is a single-operator tool — not a team platform, not a SaaS. Its closest comparators are personal infra OSS projects like Devbox, DevPod, and small CLI tooling repos, not enterprise dev platforms like Coder or Gitpod.

The decision this output informs: Rob needs to choose 2–3 concrete distribution moves to execute in the next 30–60 days. Should he write a "my private agent workstation setup" blog post or X thread? Submit to specific awesome-lists? Post a Show HN? Record a short walkthrough video? Get listed in comparison pages? The answer depends on what has actually worked for comparable projects at similar star counts (50–500 range), not on generic "OSS growth playbooks."

The ICP is: solo developers and indie consultants who run AI coding agents, want durable reproducible dev environments, already use Tailscale or SSH-first workflows, and are dissatisfied with ephemeral agent contexts. They discover new tools via GitHub trending, HN, X threads from developers they follow, and curated awesome-lists.

Good output looks like: "Projects like [X] went from 150 to 800 stars in 90 days primarily via a Show HN post that framed the problem as 'my coding agents forget everything' — here are the specific elements that worked, and here are the awesome-lists/community channels that amplified it."

**Research questions:**

1. **GitHub star-velocity case studies**: Find 5–8 OSS projects in the single-operator dev-infra / personal agent workspace / CLI tooling space (not SaaS, not team tools) that grew from ~50 to 500+ GitHub stars in 2024–2026. For each: repo name | peak growth period | primary acquisition channel | content format that triggered the spike | approximate stars/week during launch. Use Star History, HN Algolia search, and public GitHub data.

2. **Show HN effectiveness for this category**: Search HN Algolia (hn.algolia.com) for Show HN posts about personal dev infra, agent workstations, local AI tooling, CLI dev environments from 2024–2026. What point counts, comment volumes, and stated traffic spikes did the best-performing posts achieve? What made the top 5 posts work (framing, demo format, README quality, timing)?

3. **Awesome-list and curator leverage**: Which awesome-lists and GitHub curator lists (e.g., awesome-selfhosted, awesome-devenv, awesome-claude, awesome-ai-tools) include comparable tools? What traffic/star impact do inclusions typically generate? Which lists are most relevant for skillbox's niche and have open submission processes?

4. **X / Twitter dev-influencer channel**: Which X accounts with 5k–100k developer followers regularly post about personal AI tooling, agent workflows, Tailscale setups, or CLI dev environments? What content formats (threads, short demos, before/after comparisons) get the most engagement in this niche? Find 3–5 real examples with engagement data.

5. **Comparison page / alternative-to SEO**: Do comparison pages like AlternativeTo, Slant, ProductHunt, or developer-written "X vs Y" blog posts drive meaningful traffic for single-operator infra tools? What's the evidence from comparable projects (DevPod, Devbox, Daytona at early stage)? Is "skillbox vs Coder/Gitpod/Devbox" content a realistic SEO play given domain authority constraints?

6. **Founder-led / build-in-public mechanics**: For OSS projects at this stage (sub-500 stars, solo founder), what does effective "build in public" look like in 2026 — weekly changelogs on X, GitHub Discussion updates, Discord community building, or something else? Find 3–5 real examples from 2024–2026 with measurable outcomes.

7. **GEO / AI-search citation opportunity**: What queries do developers use in ChatGPT, Perplexity, or Google when looking for "private coding agent workstation", "self-hosted dev environment for AI agents", "Claude Code home directory setup", "Tailscale dev box"? Which sources currently appear in AI overviews for these? What content format (README, blog post, docs page, comparison page) consistently earns LLM citations for CLI dev tools?

**Output format:**

### Star-Velocity Case Studies
One block per project: Project | Stars then → now | Growth period | Primary channel | Content trigger | Stars/week peak | Source.

### Show HN Effectiveness
Table: Post title | Points | Comments | Stated traffic/star impact | Key success factors. Plus 2–3 paragraph synthesis.

### Awesome-List / Curator Map
Table: List name | Estimated monthly views | Stars impact per inclusion (evidence or inferred) | Relevance to skillbox | Open submissions?.

### X Influencer Map
Table: Account | Followers | Niche | Best-performing content format | Engagement benchmarks.

### Comparison Page / SEO Viability
2–3 paragraphs on whether this is worth pursuing at skillbox's current stage, with evidence.

### Build-in-Public Playbook
Ranked list of 3–5 tactics with evidence sources and confidence flags.

### GEO / AI-Search Opportunity
5–8 specific query strings with: current top cited source | content gap | recommended content format.

### Prioritized 30–60 Day Distribution Plan
Top 3 moves ranked by (reach × conversion) / effort, each with a one-sentence rationale and confidence flag.

**Hard constraints:**
- No generic OSS growth listicles. Ban Orbit, Common Room, DEV.to generic guides, and generic "how to market your OSS project" posts as sources.
- All star counts, HN points, and engagement numbers must be cited or marked "inferred/not verified."
- Focus exclusively on single-operator infra tools, not team SaaS, not enterprise dev platforms.
- Do not cover product architecture, build-vs-buy, or private agent infra adoption (Round 1 territory).
- Use primary sources: GitHub, HN Algolia, Reddit, Star History, X search.
- Flag all confidence levels explicitly.

**Completion criteria:**
1. Star-velocity case studies cover at least 5 real projects with cited data.
2. Show HN section covers at least 5 real posts with point/comment data.
3. Awesome-list map has at least 5 lists with relevance assessment.
4. GEO section has at least 5 query strings with source analysis.
5. Prioritized plan gives exactly 3 moves with confidence flags.
close_reason: Completed sourced report at docs/research/2026-06-01-oss-cli-distribution-research.md; evidence gaps: GitHub traffic/referrals, awesome-list referral impact, primary X engagement/follower metrics, and AI-search citation behavior were not publicly verifiable.

title: Deep research: What is the real adoption curve for Tailscale-first private agent infrastructure vs managed cloud agent runtimes?
description:
**Why this matters for skillbox:** skillbox's entire architectural bet — Tailscale as the access primitive, one durable private machine, operator-owned infrastructure — only wins if there is a durable cohort of developers who (a) are building seriously with AI coding agents, (b) prefer SSH + private VPS over browser IDEs and managed sandboxes, and (c) will not be swept into a Coder/Daytona/E2B managed offering as those mature. If Tailscale adoption among developer-infra builders is flat or regressing (or if Cloudflare Tunnel is eating the "private developer infra" niche), skillbox's distribution assumptions break. This research would directly shape the Phase 1–3 roadmap priority and whether to bet more on the Tailscale-native identity/access model or treat it as an optional transport.

---

## Deep research prompt

You are a developer-infrastructure analyst. Your sole task is to assess the adoption trajectory of Tailscale (and comparable private-network tools) among AI-agent developers and coding-assistant users, and to compare this against the managed-cloud-runtime trajectory (E2B, Daytona, GitHub Codespaces agent mode, Cursor hosted). You do NOT evaluate financial performance, investment prospects, or general enterprise IT Tailscale deployments outside the developer-tool context.

### Context

A CLI-first project called skillbox is built around the thesis that serious independent AI-coding-agent operators want a durable, single-tenant, Tailscale-connected private machine rather than ephemeral managed sandboxes or browser IDEs. The Tailscale access layer is not cosmetic — it drives how skill distribution, shared-box collaborator access (shared-jam), and remote runtime invocations work.

The research exists because this design has a key external dependency: the developer community using Tailscale as infrastructure for their own tooling must be large enough and growing fast enough that tooling built for it has a real addressable base. If the community is tiny or stagnating while managed offerings grow, the distribution path changes. If Tailscale is winning hard (developer blog posts, Homebrew installs, Discord communities, job postings requiring Tailscale), the bet is reinforced.

The audience for this research is the project maintainer deciding whether to deepen Tailscale-native features (richer node tagging, exit-node routing, ACL-based skill access), add a Cloudflare/WireGuard compatibility layer, or accept Tailscale as a project-scope constraint and focus only on users who have already adopted it.

Good research here means: concrete adoption evidence (install counts, developer blog references, open-source project dependencies, community size signals) for Tailscale in the developer-infra segment specifically, alongside competing private-network tools and the managed-cloud alternatives, so the project can calibrate how big its natural user base is.

### Research Questions

1. What is the current evidence for Tailscale adoption specifically among developer-infra and AI-coding-agent users? (Look for: Tailscale blog references to developer workflows, open-source projects on GitHub that list Tailscale as a dependency or in their README/docs, community Discord/Reddit presence, developer blog posts with significant engagement.)
2. What competing private-networking approaches (WireGuard raw, Cloudflare Tunnel, ZeroTier, Netbird, Headscale self-hosted) are developers in the AI-agent/coding-tool space actually using, and what are their stated reasons for choosing one over another?
3. What is the current feature gap and pricing comparison between Tailscale free/personal tier and the next tier up? Has this changed in 2024–2026 in ways that would push solo developers away or toward it?
4. What is the published adoption data or analyst estimates for E2B, Daytona, GitHub Codespaces (for agent use), and Cursor hosted environments among independent developers versus enterprise teams? Which cohort is growing faster?
5. Are there public developer surveys (Stack Overflow Developer Survey, JetBrains State of Developer Ecosystem, GitHub Octoverse, or similar) that capture data on remote development environment preferences or AI-coding-agent infrastructure preferences?
6. What do high-signal developer community threads (Hacker News "Ask HN", lobste.rs, r/selfhosted, r/devops) say about the tradeoff between "managed ephemeral" vs "private durable" infrastructure for AI coding agents?

### Output Format

**Executive Summary** (3–5 bullets on the most decision-relevant findings about Tailscale adoption and the managed-vs-private tradeoff)

**Tailscale Adoption Evidence**
- Quantitative signals: install estimates, GitHub dependency count, community size
- Qualitative signals: developer blog post themes, forum sentiment
- Trend direction (growing / flat / unknown) with confidence rating

**Competing Private-Network Tools** (one entry each for WireGuard DIY, Cloudflare Tunnel, Netbird, ZeroTier, Headscale)
- What it is and who uses it
- Evidence of developer-infra adoption
- Key reasons developers cite for choosing it over Tailscale
- Confidence: [high / medium / low]

**Managed Agent Runtime Adoption**
- E2B: evidence of adoption level, target user segment, growth signals
- Daytona: same
- GitHub Codespaces agent mode: same
- Cursor hosted / other: same
- Cross-entity synthesis: is the managed tier winning among solo devs, enterprise, or both?

**Survey and Report Data**
- Any developer surveys that address remote dev environment preferences (2024–2026)
- What they say about private-box vs managed preferences

**Developer Community Sentiment**
- 3–5 representative thread summaries (HN, Reddit, lobste.rs) on the managed-vs-private question, with URLs and engagement counts

**Uncertainty Log**
- All claims marked "not found" or "inferred" with confidence notes

### Hard Constraints

- Authoritative sources only: Tailscale official blog, GitHub repositories, credible developer press (The New Stack, InfoQ, Ars Technica, Hacker News with high engagement), official survey reports (Stack Overflow, JetBrains, GitHub Octoverse). Do NOT cite Wikipedia, Crunchbase, G2, or tool-comparison listicle sites as primary evidence.
- Every quantitative claim (install count, GitHub star count, pricing tier detail) must be cited or marked "not found / inferred."
- Do not fabricate blog post titles, forum thread URLs, or survey statistics.
- Scope: developer-infra and AI-agent tooling only. General enterprise Tailscale deployments, gaming VPNs, and home-network uses are out of scope unless they cross into the developer-tool community.
- Flag all uncertain claims with "confidence: low, needs human review."
- No marketing prose; produce an analyst's evidence brief.

### Completion Criteria

Report is complete when:
1. All six research questions have answers or explicit "not found" notes.
2. Tailscale adoption evidence section has at least three quantitative or semi-quantitative signals.
3. At least four competing private-network tools have per-tool entries.
4. At least three managed agent runtimes have adoption evidence with trend direction.
5. All sources are cited inline or in a reference list.
close_reason: Completed dated developer-infra research artifact with Tailscale adoption evidence, private-network competitors, managed agent runtime comparison, survey data, community sentiment, uncertainty log, and validation checks.

title: Build dev-to-prod parity compiler report
description:
Smart-goal bead for idea #3 from the 2026-05-12 idea-wizard pass: build a dev-to-prod parity compiler for Skillbox.

The idea: turn the roadmap's prod-bridge thesis into an executable `parity-report`/planner surface that compares client overlay services, env files, health checks, reverse-proxy roles, deploy-skill modes, and production-stack declarations. The output should name exact missing pieces and generate reviewable preview artifacts, so Skillbox becomes a staging-grade proving layer without turning into a hosted platform or replacing app-specific CI/deploy systems.
design:
repo_state:
  date: 2026-05-12
  head: 0691013 fix(skills): prefer path-specific scope rules
  branch: main ahead origin/main by 6 at bead creation time
  dirty_paths_at_creation: .beads/issues.jsonl, .env-manager/runtime_manager/cli.py, .gitignore, README.md, scripts/sbp, tests/test_cli_wrappers.py
context:
  source_idea: idea-wizard ranked idea #3, Dev-to-prod parity compiler
  docs_anchor: docs/ROADMAP.md Phase 2 says Skillbox is missing the bridge to the operator's nginx/deploy layer, including reverse-proxy parity, env parity, and deploy skill integration.
  implemented_surface: runtime graph, local runtime profiles, parity ledger validation, env files, client-project/open/publish, stewardship-report, and agent-readable capabilities already exist.
  open_beads: skillbox-agm broad audit/smart-goal bead; skillbox-o7j MCP extras review bead.
integrity_gap:
  The README and roadmap describe parity concepts, but there is no single inspectable command that answers whether a client overlay is actually ready to behave like a staging/devbox tier for a real prod stack.
hardening_decision:
  hardening-first by extension: build on runtime graph, parity ledger, env files, client projection, and stewardship-report instead of inventing a new deployment subsystem.
out_of_scope:
  - Do not build a hosted distributor, hosted control plane, browser IDE, or generic deploy product.
  - Do not mutate production infrastructure.
  - Do not require live nginx, registry, cloud, or Tailscale credentials for the default proof path.
  - Do not hide app-specific deploy decisions inside Skillbox; generate declarations, reports, and safe previews.
acceptance_criteria:
/goal $domain-planner $describe $reproduce process complete

One-shot goal: Design and implement the first Skillbox dev-to-prod parity compiler slice: a read-only parity report/planner that compares declared client runtime surfaces against a production-stack contract and reports reverse-proxy, env, healthcheck, and deploy-mode drift with machine-readable JSON, concise text output, and regression proof.

Success criteria:
- Start from the existing runtime graph, parity ledger, env_files, service healthcheck, local runtime mode, client projection, and stewardship-report concepts; do not create a separate deployment platform.
- Define a minimal production-stack contract that can live in a client overlay or adjacent private config and can represent reverse-proxy routes/upstreams, env var expectations, health endpoints, deploy skill modes, and optional Docker network assumptions.
- Add a read-only CLI surface with JSON output and safe first-try guidance, tentatively `python3 .env-manager/manage.py parity-report <client> --format json`, plus an MCP/tooling path if it fits existing runtime-manager patterns.
- The report must classify `ready`, `missing`, `drift`, `deferred`, and `not_assessed` domains; each finding must include the source declaration, the expected prod contract, and the next safe command or file to edit.
- Include at least one fixture client that proves the happy path and at least one fixture that proves drift across proxy, env, healthcheck, and deploy-mode domains.
- Integrate the summary into `stewardship-report` or explicitly document why the first slice keeps it separate.
- Update README/docs/ROADMAP so implemented behavior, limitations, and non-goals are separated from future roadmap prose.
- Verification must include focused unit tests for the parity planner/report, CLI JSON/text behavior, fixture drift cases, `git diff --check`, `make doctor`, and `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` unless a concrete environment blocker is recorded.
- Detached fresh-eyes review is required before completion, and this goal authorizes at least one scoped detached reviewer subagent for that gate, plus additional detached review rounds as needed: launch or route an independent reviewer that did not implement the work, have it inspect the final diff/artifacts/validation evidence, and address or explicitly block on its findings before marking the goal complete.
- Completion means validate -> commit only when authorized -> post-commit reality check; if commit authorization, credentials, or external prod fixtures block closeout, report the exact resume condition rather than calling it done.
notes:
chain_context: Builds on the idea-wizard #3 recommendation and the existing Skillbox roadmap, while avoiding duplication of the closed agent-ergonomics/SBP/MCP goal beads.
modes: no overlay-backed modes report was present for this repo at creation time.
crap_status: unknown fresh baseline for the future parity-report implementation; run CRAP only if the implementation touches enough supported Python hotspot scope to make the score meaningful.
skill_combo: domain-planner to shape the contract, describe to pin pass/fail cases, reproduce for command-first verification.
first_step: read docs/ROADMAP.md Phase 2 plus the current runtime graph/env_files/parity-ledger validation code, then draft the smallest production-stack contract fixture before coding the report.
loop_goal: make Skillbox able to prove whether a client devbox surface matches the operator's prod-stack shape closely enough to be a staging-grade proving layer.
success_criteria_summary: read-only report/planner, explicit prod contract, drift classification, fixture coverage, docs update, focused and broad validation, detached review, commit/reality-check gate.
resume_condition: start implementation by defining the production-stack contract shape and fixture matrix; another /smart pass is useful after the report exists or if contract placement becomes ambiguous.
completion_gate: pending; this is a goal contract, not an implementation completion claim.
smart_goal_mode: compact-goal
smart_goal_contract: |
  /goal $domain-planner $describe $reproduce process complete
  
  One-shot goal: Design and implement the first Skillbox dev-to-prod parity compiler slice: a read-only parity report/planner that compares declared client runtime surfaces against a production-stack contract and reports reverse-proxy, env, healthcheck, and deploy-mode drift with machine-readable JSON, concise text output, and regression proof.
  
  Success criteria:
  - Start from the existing runtime graph, parity ledger, env_files, service healthcheck, local runtime mode, client projection, and stewardship-report concepts; do not create a separate deployment platform.
  - Define a minimal production-stack contract that can live in a client overlay or adjacent private config and can represent reverse-proxy routes/upstreams, env var expectations, health endpoints, deploy skill modes, and optional Docker network assumptions.
  - Add a read-only CLI surface with JSON output and safe first-try guidance, tentatively `python3 .env-manager/manage.py parity-report <client> --format json`, plus an MCP/tooling path if it fits existing runtime-manager patterns.
  - The report must classify `ready`, `missing`, `drift`, `deferred`, and `not_assessed` domains; each finding must include the source declaration, the expected prod contract, and the next safe command or file to edit.
  - Include at least one fixture client that proves the happy path and at least one fixture that proves drift across proxy, env, healthcheck, and deploy-mode domains.
  - Integrate the summary into `stewardship-report` or explicitly document why the first slice keeps it separate.
  - Update README/docs/ROADMAP so implemented behavior, limitations, and non-goals are separated from future roadmap prose.
  - Verification must include focused unit tests for the parity planner/report, CLI JSON/text behavior, fixture drift cases, `git diff --check`, `make doctor`, and `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` unless a concrete environment blocker is recorded.
  - Detached fresh-eyes review is required before completion, and this goal authorizes at least one scoped detached reviewer subagent for that gate, plus additional detached review rounds as needed: launch or route an independent reviewer that did not implement the work, have it inspect the final diff/artifacts/validation evidence, and address or explicitly block on its findings before marking the goal complete.
  - Completion means validate -> commit only when authorized -> post-commit reality check; if commit authorization, credentials, or external prod fixtures block closeout, report the exact resume condition rather than calling it done.
close_reason: Implemented and committed as 6eef967; remaining broad validation blockers recorded as local-state/environmental.

comments.author: b
comments.text: Implementation evidence 2026-05-12: added read-only parity-report CLI/MCP surface, production_stack overlay contract preservation, dev/prod parity summary in stewardship-report, docs updates, and fixture regressions for ready, drift, no-contract, cwd inference, MCP input, and service_mode_commands client scoping. Detached reviewer 019e1e51-c203-7201-bb1e-e8dc0f13470b found four issues; all addressed. Verification: py_compile targeted files; focused parity/CLI/MCP/runtime-model tests; real parity-report personal --format json (ready=false, status=not_ready, blocking_count=0, contract_present=false for current private overlay); git diff --check; make doctor; make dev-sanity (existing skill-repo-install warning only); python3 -m unittest discover -s tests => 926 tests, 1 skipped. Closeout blocker: commit/post-commit reality check still requires explicit commit authorization.

comments.text: Closeout after current run: committed implementation as 6eef967 feat(runtime): add parity reporting. Refreshed evidence: git diff --check passed; py_compile targeted runtime files passed; focused unittest lane tests.test_runtime_manager tests.test_cli_wrappers tests.test_mcp_server tests.test_mmdx_open tests.test_runtime_hotspot_units tests.test_cli_units passed earlier in this run (353 tests); R-001/R-002 agent ergonomics regressions passed after R-002 expectation update.
