- Do not change agent homes or global MCP configs outside this repo unless explicitly required and documented.
- Do not run infra-starting commands.
close_reason: Made MCP parity intentional and enforceable. mcp_visibility.collect_mcp_audit now takes a declared-servers baseline (all kind:mcp services in the full, unfiltered runtime model, gated to self-audit); cli passes it via _full_declared_mcp_servers. cm/dcg/fwc are declared services behind memory/connectors profiles, so they are reported as intentional (extra_intentional / parity.claude_only_declared) rather than drift. Only undeclared servers count as summary.unexplained_drift, and next_actions only nag on those. mcp-audit --cwd . --format json now reports unexplained_drift=0 with empty next_actions. No MCP server added; no secrets/host paths introduced. Focused tests added in tests/test_mcp_visibility.py (declared-intentional + foreign-repo isolation); make doctor, make dev-sanity, test_mcp_server, test_cli_units, git diff --check pass.

title: Build read-only runtime evidence packet
description:
Source: h1k.1 manual reality proof, h1k.2/h1k.3 accepted slate, and h1k.4 profiling/refactor plan. Future agents need a repeatable CLI/JSON evidence packet instead of manually stitching doctor/status/pressure/pulse/skills/MCP outputs.

title: Run post-SBP reality check and smart goal
description:

The point is not a brainstorm. Compare implemented code, docs, plans, Beads, and validation commands against the project vision; then write the single most accretive smart-goal contract for the next move. If the pass discovers truly accretive follow-up work, create/update Beads with evidence and dependencies.
design:
concern: post-visibility project truth and next smart goal
writes: Beads issue notes/children and overlay-backed smart-chain artifacts if the repo has a matching invocation root
non_goals: broad brainstorm; implementation before the reality check names the next move

acceptance_criteria:

comments.author: b
comments.text:
Reality check 2026-05-11: README/VISION promise is one private Tailnet/Docker dev box with durable state, explicit runtime graphs, repo overlays, agent context, skill visibility, MCP/operator tools, Swimmers integration, worker/session history, and low ceremony. Current implemented proof: `make doctor` passed 13/0/0; `make dev-sanity` passed 23 checks with 1 warning; SBP repo-local skill view is clean with 0 missing_for_cwd, 0 scope_violations, 0 broken_project; MCP expected `skillbox` and `skillbox-operator` are present in Claude and Codex; `manage.py capabilities --json` exposes `swimmers-launch`; `manage.py swimmers-launch ... --dry-run --format json` and `scripts/sbp launch ... --dry-run --json` both produced parseable dry-run payloads; focused `python3 -m unittest tests.test_swimmers_launch tests.test_cli_units tests.test_cli_wrappers tests.test_mcp_visibility` ran 40 tests OK; `git diff --check` passed. Current gaps: current Swimmers launch surface is uncommitted and only dry-run proven, no non-dry-run POST was attempted to avoid spawning agents; full `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` is not a clean proof because it hung in `tests/test_install_script.py::test_verify_runs_post_install_checks` inside `install.sh --verify -> manage.py first-box`; `make dev-sanity` still warns about extra default/global installed skills (`cass`, `cass-memory`, `changelog-md-workmanship`, `ga4`, `readme-writing`, `smart`) which I treated as out-of-scope global drift; ignored local MCP extras `cm`, `dcg`, `fwc` remain operator-local review items; live DigitalOcean/Tailscale provisioning proof is still external. No overlay plan index or released plan tree exists under the resolved skillbox client overlay, so Beads are the active plan surface. Existing broad Pass 2 smart goal remains directionally right, but the concrete next hardening gate is full-suite determinism before any claim that the new agent surface is complete.

Smart goal contract:
/goal $reproduce $installer-workmanship process complete

One-shot goal: Make Skillbox full repo validation deterministic again by diagnosing and fixing the `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` hang at `tests/test_install_script.py::test_verify_runs_post_install_checks` / `install.sh --verify -> manage.py first-box`, then rerun the current Swimmers batch-launch surface closeout from a clean full-suite baseline.

Success criteria:
- Reproduce the install verification hang with a bounded command and capture where `install.sh`, `first-box`, or acceptance is waiting.
- Fix the root cause without weakening real installer verification; if the test needs a tighter harness path, prove production `--verify` behavior remains meaningful.
- Preserve current repo-local SBP/MCP state and do not prune broad global/default skill drift as part of this goal.
- Keep the Swimmers launch surface scoped: `manage.py swimmers-launch`, `scripts/sbp launch/bulk`, README docs, capabilities, and focused tests stay green.
- Focused tests pass: `python3 -m unittest tests.test_swimmers_launch tests.test_cli_units tests.test_cli_wrappers tests.test_mcp_visibility tests.test_install_script`.
- Broad proof passes or blocks explicitly: `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests`, `make doctor`, `make dev-sanity`, and `git diff --check`.
- Completion gate is validate -> commit only with explicit authorization -> post-commit reality check; absent commit authorization, report commit-blocked rather than complete.
- Detached fresh-eyes review is required before completion and may be a scoped reviewer pass on the final diff and validation evidence; do not launch one per bead.

Loop goal: restore trust in Skillbox validation and make the current agent-facing Swimmers launch slice closeable.
Resume condition: start the next implementation pass by reproducing the install verification hang with a bounded command; another `/smart` pass is useful only after full-suite determinism is restored or the blocker is proven external.
Completion gate: pending.

title: Pressure-aware Skillbox build offload and storage guard
description:
End vision: Skillbox becomes pressure-aware for agent-heavy coding work. Local agents get explicit disk-pressure posture, large build/test commands can route to non-production remote workers through remote_compilation_helper, and storage_ballast_helper protects the operator machine and selected Skillbox boxes from disk-full incidents without touching protected agent history or production Sweet Potato infrastructure.

Default target from current evidence is the managed non-production Tailnet box `portfolio-devbox` (`skillbox-portfolio-devbox`, <tailnet-address>), not `jeremy`, not `ssh-info`, and not `sweet-potato-prod`. All destructive cleanup/install/deploy work must remain blocked until the target and policy are reverified in a child issue.
acceptance_criteria:
Epic is complete only when:
- Skillbox has a read-only pressure/build-offload report that can name disk pressure, protected paths, cleanup candidates, RCH posture, and SBH posture.
- RCH and SBH are optional managed artifacts/services/checks in the runtime graph, with safe disabled-by-default behavior.
- Agent context/focus/stewardship surfaces tell agents when to avoid local builds and how to route expensive build/test commands.
- `portfolio-devbox` is validated as the non-production Tailnet target before any remote install/deploy/probe.
- `jeremy`, `ssh-info`, and `sweet-potato-prod` are explicitly excluded from write/deploy actions.
- Tests/docs are updated and repo-native validation passes or concrete blockers are recorded.
close_reason: Epic acceptance met. Skillbox now has read-only pressure-report/rch-report/sbh-report surfaces; pressure-tools optional RCH/SBH artifacts, services, checks, env defaults, compose env, and dependency inventory; pressure/offload advisory in status, compact status, generated agent context, focus/stewardship summaries, and pulse state; docs and robot discovery updated. portfolio-devbox was revalidated as the non-production Tailnet target before closeout, and jeremy/ssh-info/sweet-potato-prod remain explicit exclusions. Validation: 66 focused tests OK, git diff --check OK, capabilities/robot-docs/status/render OK, br dep cycles count 0. make doctor/dev-sanity blockers recorded as existing stale skill installs only. No cleanup/delete/prod mutation/commit performed.

title: Add Skillbox RCH topology adapter/staging lane
description: Build the durable no-sudo path for running <workspace> projects through RCH on portfolio-devbox. Current evidence shows RCH selection/SSH/rustup work, but upstream RCH uses one path_topology policy for local and worker and maps sync roots to /data/projects, which portfolio-devbox cannot create without root. Candidate implementation: Skillbox-managed staging lane that mirrors a requested repo into a topology-compatible scratch tree, sets RCH env/config per invocation, and/or provides an explicit path-translation adapter for SSH/rsync. Acceptance: no deletion by default, no protected local state, no production boxes, manifest of staged paths, proof that rch exec remote_count increments for a canary, and documented rollback/cleanup commands left operator-controlled.

title: No-ragrets push readiness closeout for 38 unpushed Skillbox commits
description:
Future success: origin/main contains the 38 local commits plus any required closeout fixes, and a fresh clone can trust the pushed tree.

Outcomes:
- All current dirty/untracked artifacts are either committed intentionally or removed/reworked before push.
- The broken install verification lane is fixed or explicitly quarantined with a narrower, documented command; no temp runtime process keeps .skillbox-state busy after tests.
- Existing wave-4 no-ragrets hardening children are closed or intentionally deferred with evidence.
- Full repo validation, secret/generated-noise scan, and independent reviewer pass before push.
- Push happens only after validation/review evidence is current.

Failure avoided: pushing an open-source repo with broken internal references, leaking local/private state, a failing install test, or unreviewed agent-generated artifacts.
design:
write_scope: repo-wide closeout, but code edits must be owned by child Beads
stop_rules:
  - Do not push while tests are red, dirty artifacts are unexplained, or reviewer has unresolved blockers.
  - Do not delete or revert user/agent work without first classifying it as generated noise or moving it into a committed/closed Bead.
  - Do not include secrets, private overlays, .env/.env.box, workspace/secrets, or runtime state.
validation_order:
  1. close concrete blockers
  2. sync Beads
  3. full validation
  4. independent reviewer
  5. commit
  6. push
acceptance_criteria: Done only when: git status is clean before final push; br ready is empty or any remaining open issues are deliberately deferred and not push blockers; git diff --check, make doctor, make dev-sanity, python3 -m unittest discover -s tests, MCP audit, and secret/noise scan pass; an independent subagent review reports no push blockers; commits are created; git push succeeds and branch is no longer ahead of origin/main.
close_reason: Push readiness closeout complete. All child blockers closed; full validation passed; independent reviewer reported no push blockers; main readiness commit b8db898 pushed to origin/main. Final Beads bookkeeping will be synced, committed, and pushed as part of this closeout.

title: Fix install --verify test leak before push
description: Push blocker: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests hung in tests.test_install_script.InstallScriptTests.test_verify_runs_post_install_checks. Focused rerun failed after 159s with OSError Errno 66 Directory not empty: temp skillbox/.skillbox-state. Evidence showed install.sh --verify ran first-box -> acceptance and left a pulse runtime process with cwd inside the temp checkout, keeping cleanup busy.\n\nFix direction: make install verification/tests deterministic and self-cleaning. Prefer no-infra/no-daemon behavior for installer tests or explicit teardown of processes launched under a temp repo. Do not weaken real first-box acceptance semantics for users.
design:
writes:
  - install.sh
  - tests/test_install_script.py
  - maybe .env-manager/runtime_manager/workflows.py only if root cause is product-side process lifecycle
stop_rules:
  - If fixing requires changing real first-box acceptance behavior, split a product bug and keep installer test coverage explicit.
  - Do not hide failures by deleting the test or removing --verify coverage.
validation:
  - python3 -m unittest tests.test_install_script.InstallScriptTests.test_verify_runs_post_install_checks
  - python3 -m unittest tests.test_install_script
  - ps/lsof check for temp-root processes when diagnosing
acceptance_criteria: Focused test passes: python3 -m unittest tests.test_install_script.InstallScriptTests.test_verify_runs_post_install_checks. Full install-script module passes. No process remains with cwd under the temp repo after the test. Broad unittest can pass through this test without hanging. git diff --check clean.
close_reason: Reworked installer tests to use a minimal fake source/manage.py fixture instead of invoking real first-box/acceptance from unit tests. Validation: python3 -m py_compile tests/test_install_script.py; python3 -m unittest tests.test_install_script.InstallScriptTests.test_verify_runs_post_install_checks; python3 -m unittest tests.test_install_script; git diff --check. Process check found no install.sh/first-box/acceptance/pulse processes left by the run.

title: Package planning docs and new golden tests intentionally for OSS push
description: Current dirty/untracked push blockers: docs/status/ has four planning artifacts referenced by diagrams/skillbox-project-status.mmdx; tests/goldens/context plus tests/test_context_drift_goldens.py and tests/test_worker_broker_smoke.py are untracked even though their Beads are closed.\n\nFix direction: decide and execute the correct OSS packaging. Commit artifacts if they are intended public evidence/tests; sanitize or rework stale/private/local content; remove references if docs should not ship. Do not leave broken references or untracked closed-Bead work.
design:
writes:
  - docs/status/** if public planning artifacts are kept
  - diagrams/skillbox-project-status.mmdx if references need rework
  - tests/test_context_drift_goldens.py
  - tests/test_worker_broker_smoke.py
  - tests/goldens/context/**
stop_rules:
  - Do not commit .skillbox-state, logs, temp run dirs, private client overlays, or secrets.
  - If a planning artifact is too stale or private, either sanitize it or remove its diagram reference.
validation:
  - python3 -m unittest tests.test_context_drift_goldens tests.test_worker_broker_smoke
  - rg targeted privacy/secret scan over docs/status and tests/goldens
acceptance_criteria: git status shows no untracked docs/status, tests/goldens, test_context_drift_goldens, or test_worker_broker_smoke paths. diagrams/skillbox-project-status.mmdx references existing tracked files. OSS/privacy scan of these artifacts finds no secrets, private overlays, .env values, or inappropriate local runtime state. Focused tests for newly tracked test modules pass.
close_reason: Kept docs/status planning artifacts and context/worker smoke goldens as intentional OSS artifacts. Sanitized dated docs to remove local absolute/private wiki path details and mark them as 2026-05-28 evidence, not current truth. Validation: python3 -m unittest tests.test_context_drift_goldens tests.test_worker_broker_smoke; targeted secret/path scan over docs/status, tests/goldens, and new test modules; git diff --check for those paths.

title: Finish or defer existing wave-4 no-ragrets blockers before push
description: Existing wave-4 graph has open/in-progress hardening work: pressure formatter, evidence assembler, event-feed redaction, worker-runtime flake, and the wave-4 epic. These affect files/tests in the current push window.\n\nFix direction: close implemented work with evidence, finish real push blockers, or deliberately defer non-push refactors. Do not push with open ready work that touches the same release surface unless it is intentionally out of scope and not dirty.
design:
writes:
  - .beads/issues.jsonl
  - files owned by the specific wave-4 child if finishing it
stop_rules:
  - Do not merge incomplete refactors just to close the epic.
  - Do not leave source/test files dirty for deferred work.
  - Security redaction gaps beat refactor cleanup for push readiness.
validation:
  - br ready --json
  - br list --status=open --json
  - br dep cycles --json
acceptance_criteria: br list --status=open shows no ready wave-4 issue that is a push blocker. If pressure/evidence refactors are deferred, they are marked deferred/open with notes explaining why they do not block this push and dirty files from them are absent. Event-feed redaction and worker-runtime flake are fixed or explicitly classified as non-blocking with evidence. br dep cycles empty.
close_reason: All wave-4 blockers affecting this push are closed or resolved with evidence: A pressure formatter closed, B evidence assembler closed as no-edit/isomorphic, E1 event-feed redaction fixed, F worker runtime flake fixed, G install-test leak fixed through push-readiness bead. br dep cycles returned count=0; br ready only showed the wave-4 epic and this gate before close.

title: Run final validation and secret/noise scan for push
description: Final local proof before reviewer/push. This gates the branch after code/docs/test fixes are in place.\n\nFix direction: run the project gates and targeted OSS scans from a clean intentional tree. Record exact failing commands as new child Beads rather than hand-waving. This issue should remain blocked until install verification, artifact packaging, and wave-4 closeout are done.
design:
dependencies:
  - skillbox-push-readiness-closeout-u0e.1
  - skillbox-push-readiness-closeout-u0e.2
  - skillbox-push-readiness-closeout-u0e.3
stop_rules:
  - Any reproducible failing command creates/fixes a child blocker before proceeding to review.
  - Do not run destructive infra/provisioning.
  - If secret scanner binary is unavailable, run targeted regex diff scan and report the limitation.
acceptance_criteria: Passes: git diff --check; bash -n install.sh scripts/07-build-and-push-binary.sh scripts/guard-destructive-op.sh scripts/sbp; make doctor; make dev-sanity; python3 .env-manager/manage.py mcp-audit --cwd <repo> --format json with unexplained_drift=0; python3 -m unittest discover -s tests; targeted secret/noise scan over origin/main..HEAD and untracked-to-be-added files; git status has only intentional staged/unstaged changes before commit.
close_reason: Completed final push-readiness gates: git diff --check; bash -n install/release scripts; make doctor; make dev-sanity with only known undeclared extra-skill warning; MCP audit unexplained_drift=0; full python3 -m unittest discover -s tests passed 1089 tests with 1 skipped; diff-focused path/secret scan clean.

title: Independent reviewer signoff and recursive fix loop before push
description: User requested an independent reviewer subagent before push. This issue gates final commit/push after local validation.\n\nFix direction: launch one focused reviewer subagent over the final diff and validation evidence. Treat reviewer findings as push blockers unless they are clearly false positives with evidence. Recursively fix and re-review until no blockers remain, then commit and push.
design:
dependencies:
  - skillbox-push-readiness-closeout-u0e.4
reviewer_contract:
  - code-review stance: bugs, regressions, secrets/private data, generated noise, broken docs, missing tests
  - do not edit files from reviewer unless explicitly asked; reviewer returns findings
stop_rules:
  - Do not push with unresolved reviewer blockers.
  - Do not push with dirty worktree after commit.
  - If remote push rejects, fetch/rebase only after inspecting remote change risk.
acceptance_criteria: Reviewer receives current diff/status/validation evidence and reports no Critical/High push blockers. Any reviewer blocker is fixed and revalidated before commit. Final commit(s) include all intended code/docs/tests/Beads; br sync --flush-only has run; git status clean after commit; git push origin main succeeds; git status --branch no longer reports ahead commits.
close_reason: Independent reviewer subagent reported no blocking findings. Residual notes were non-blocking existing Beads/local-path hygiene and a pre-existing fixture path on origin/main; new wave-4 Beads notes were scrubbed before commit. Main readiness commit b8db898 pushed to origin/main successfully.

title: Enforce Read-before-Edit in loop/smart skill templates
description:
Friction: The Edit tool requires a prior Read of the target file, but agents in /loop /smart cycles jump straight to editing files identified from scan output without reading them first. In session e8ee60ce (10,641 lines, 2,727 tool calls), this happened 32 times across 31 unique .tsx files — 64 wasted tool invocations (error + Read + retry). The agent identifies targets from jq output, then calls Edit without Read, gets rejected, has to Read, then retry.

Cass evidence: session e8ee60ce lines L2757-L10312 — 32 Edit-without-Read errors, each followed by Read + retry. 64 wasted tool calls in a single session.

Lube fix (smallest durable unblocker): Add a Read-before-Edit invariant to the /smart loop template or drift-detector skill. When the skill identifies a file to modify, emit a Read call before the Edit. This is a one-line change to the skill's planning prompt or template.

No-ragrets contract:
- Outcome: /loop /smart cycles never waste tool calls on Edit-without-Read errors.
- Evidence: 32 occurrences (64 wasted calls) in one session — the most frequent single friction point observed.
- Failure avoided: agent burns 2 extra tool calls per file modification in a loop that touches 100+ files.
- Acceptance: (1) smart/loop template includes Read-before-Edit guidance, (2) drift-detector skill emits Read before Edit in its action plan, (3) zero Edit-without-Read errors in next /loop /smart run.
close_reason: Added Read-before-Edit invariant to smart and loop skill templates

title: Redact subprocess stderr before MCP/runtime event exposure
description:
timestamp: 2026-05-29T19:34:34-07:00
repo_head: 8143a9c
compared_to: skillbox-smart-20260518-validation-trust-gkn
modes_report: none consulted
modes_takeaway: latest closed smart link restored validation trust on 2026-05-29; this audit found a new security hardening gap in MCP/operator diagnostic redaction after that baseline.
delta_since_last_chain: current worktree had only pre-existing untracked docs/status before this audit; this handoff adds .beads/issues.jsonl only.
repo_signals: .env-manager/mcp_server.py emits raw subprocess stderr into MCP notifications and stderr; scripts/operator_mcp_server.py prints child stderr and returns raw ssh stderr; tests assert raw stderr pass-through
integrity_gap: secret-like subprocess diagnostics can be copied into MCP transcripts, runtime logs, and operator tool payloads without redaction
crap_status: unknown baseline for this security surface
hardening_decision: hardening-first
move: add a shared diagnostic redaction helper for subprocess stderr/stdout before MCP notifications, operator logs, and returned remote-command payloads
skill_combo: describe -> reproduce -> targeted unit tests -> full unittest discovery
why_now: this closes the highest-impact audit finding because Skillbox routinely handles .env, .env.box, DO/Tailscale tokens, bearer tokens, and remote command output
first_step: write regression tests that feed SKILLBOX_DO_TOKEN=do-secret, Authorization: Bearer token-secret, and password=secret into the MCP/operator stderr paths and assert redacted output
next_smart_condition: rerun /smart after the redaction patch lands and the full test gate passes
loop_goal: prevent secret-bearing diagnostics from entering agent-visible Skillbox MCP and operator channels
success_criteria: tests prove raw token, password, secret, API key, and bearer values are redacted in .env-manager/mcp_server.py and scripts/operator_mcp_server.py, while non-secret diagnostics remain useful
loop_status: one-shot 1/1
resume_condition: implement the redaction patch and validate with python3 -m unittest tests.test_mcp_server tests.test_operator_mcp_server tests.test_runtime_file_units plus python3 -m unittest discover -s tests
completion_gate: pending

Describe: Redact subprocess diagnostics before exposure
Context:
- Type: bug-fix
- Affected: .env-manager/mcp_server.py:1303, scripts/operator_mcp_server.py:463, scripts/operator_mcp_server.py:578
- Current behavior: subprocess stderr and ssh failure stderr are emitted or returned verbatim.
- Desired behavior: secret-like key/value pairs, bearer tokens, and known credential env values are redacted before any MCP notification, stderr print, event log, or tool payload exposure.
- Root cause: subprocess wrapper helpers treat diagnostics as safe text.
Test cases:
- TC-1: given stderr containing SKILLBOX_DO_TOKEN=do-secret and Authorization: Bearer token-secret, when run_manage/run_script/run_ssh handle it, then exposed data contains redaction markers and not the raw values.
- TC-2: given ordinary stderr such as build failed, when the same wrappers expose diagnostics, then the actionable non-secret message remains present.
Validation commands:
- python3 -m unittest tests.test_mcp_server tests.test_operator_mcp_server tests.test_runtime_file_units
- python3 -m unittest discover -s tests
design:
No-ragrets success contract
- Outcome: Skillbox never exposes raw secret-bearing subprocess diagnostics through MCP notifications, operator MCP responses, runtime events, or local stderr mirrors.
- Evidence: Regression tests inject SKILLBOX_DO_TOKEN=do-secret, Authorization: Bearer token-secret, password=secret, api_key=secret, and a benign stderr line through the affected wrappers; outputs preserve benign diagnostics and replace secret values with stable redaction markers.
- Failure avoided: A future agent or operator does not accidentally copy DO/Tailscale/API/bearer credentials into transcripts, logs, or support handoffs while debugging failed lifecycle commands.

Nearest valuable artifact: a security hardening patch plus tests for diagnostic redaction.
Proof of improvement: before tests demonstrate raw secret values leak through current wrappers; after tests demonstrate redaction at every affected exposure point.

Write scope:
- .env-manager/mcp_server.py
- scripts/operator_mcp_server.py
- tests/test_mcp_server.py
- tests/test_operator_mcp_server.py
- optional shared helper location only if it reduces duplication without expanding the blast radius

Non-goals:
- Do not suppress all stderr; preserve actionable diagnostics.
- Do not change command execution semantics, dry-run markers, SSH argv validation, or operator auth behavior.
- Do not edit secrets, .env, .env.box, workspace/secrets, or generated runtime state.
- Do not broaden into ingress proxy/event-feed/stub-api medium findings in this bead.

Stop conditions:
- Stop and report if redaction would require changing external command output contracts beyond diagnostics.
- Stop and split a new bead if the fix touches runtime projection sanitizers, installer integrity, or unrelated lifecycle code.
- Stop if the full suite fails in a reproducible way outside the redaction scope; record exact failing tests and keep the focused redaction proof separate.

Review and hardening rules:
- Treat this as security hardening, not CLI polish.
- Prefer a small deterministic redaction helper with tests over regex sprawl at each call site.
- Include at least one regression for MCP notification payloads and one for operator SSH/script/compose-style returned payloads.
- Re-run the broad suite once; if timing flakes recur, rerun the failing tests individually and report both results.
acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance criteria
- Raw secret values are redacted from .env-manager/mcp_server.py run_manage stderr notification data and stderr mirror output.
- Raw secret values are redacted from scripts/operator_mcp_server.py run_script stderr mirror output and run_ssh returned stderr/stdout payloads.
- Existing benign stderr remains visible enough to debug ordinary command failures.
- Tests cover key/value secrets, bearer tokens, and ordinary non-secret diagnostics.
- Focused validation passes: python3 -m unittest tests.test_mcp_server tests.test_operator_mcp_server tests.test_runtime_file_units.
- Broad validation is attempted: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests. If it flakes, record the full-suite failures plus independent rerun results for the failing tests.
- Final closeout includes git diff --check and br sync --flush-only --json evidence.
close_reason: Implemented deterministic subprocess diagnostic redaction for MCP/operator exposure and validated focused plus full unittest gates.

title: Reliability: crash-consistent state and truthful lifecycle transitions
description:
EPIC: Make every durable-state write and lifecycle transition crash-consistent and truthful: locked+atomic focus/pulse state, fully-atomic reconcile outputs, honest partial-start results, API-confirmed teardown states, journaled box inventory, real MCP-bridge readiness, and bounded SSH retries.

WHY: skillbox's thesis is "durable state beats ephemeral sandboxes" — the state had better deserve the trust. The MODES report verified concurrent unlocked readers/writers of .focus.json (S4, four independent reader sites), mixed transactional semantics across reconcile outputs (S6), and teardown/inventory truthfulness risks (S5); the 2026-06 audits added cm-bridge false-readiness (D2) and missing retry discipline on flaky SSH. Pulse MAX_RESTART_ATTEMPTS (S3) was found ALREADY WIRED during June re-verification — it is covered by chaos tests in the testing epic, not re-fixed here.

SEQUENCING: locking + atomic-writes (the two foundations) land first; partial-start, teardown truth, inventory journal build on the same primitives; bridge readiness and SSH retry are independent.

EXIT: kill -9 at any point during focus/sync/reconcile/teardown leaves state that the next command reads coherently (possibly stale, never torn, never lying about infrastructure).
close_reason: done

title: S4: lock + atomic-rename discipline for focus and pulse state
description:
OBJECTIVE: Serialize and atomize all shared mutable state files — .focus.json, pulse.state.json, dryrun markers aside (own bead) — with an advisory-lock + temp-rename helper, ending the S4 race class.

BACKGROUND: MODES S4 (0.85): workspace/.focus.json has four independent readers (Makefile compose-layer resolution, 04-reconcile, operator_mcp_server, runtime_ops) and writers in workflows.py (focus) with no locking anywhere (verified 2026-06-12: zero fcntl/flock hits in the tree). pulse.state.json is written by the daemon while focus/status read it. Today's failure modes: torn reads (reader sees half-written JSON), lost updates (focus during pulse write), and the Makefile silently falling back to monoserver mounts when .focus.json is momentarily invalid — which changes WHICH FILESYSTEM gets mounted into the container.

REASONING: these files steer compose composition and agent context; a torn read does not crash, it quietly mis-steers — the worst failure shape.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Helper in runtime_manager/_shared/fs.py (or shared.py pre-split): locked_json_update(path, mutate_fn) using fcntl.flock on a sidecar .lock file + write-temp-fsync-rename; read_json_tolerant(path) that retries once on decode error (rename makes torn reads near-impossible; the retry covers NFS-ish edge cases). (2) Convert writers: workflows.py focus persist, pulse.py state snapshot, anything else writing these two files (audit first, list in PR). (3) Readers: pure reads need no lock once writes are atomic renames — document that invariant in the helper docstring so nobody adds read-locks ritualistically; the Makefile's python -c reader is fine post-rename-atomicity but its SILENT fallback gets a stderr warning line. (4) Per-agent focus files (MODES suggested) considered and DEFERRED: single-operator box, single focus is the product model; record the option here for the multi-agent future, do not build it.

FILES: shared fs helper, workflows.py, pulse.py, Makefile (warning line), tests.

RISKS: flock on filesystems without lock support (state root could be exotic) — degrade to atomic-rename-only with a one-time warning; lock contention deadlocks (always lock with timeout + fail with STATE_LOCK_TIMEOUT, never block forever).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) concurrent stress test (N processes hammering focus updates while M read) shows zero torn/lost updates; (2) kill -9 mid-write leaves either old or new content, never partial (loop test); (3) lock timeout produces STATE_LOCK_TIMEOUT with holder info if readable; (4) Makefile fallback now warns on stderr; (5) all suite green.

TESTING: multiprocessing stress test (marked slow-ok, bounded seconds); kill-during-write loop; lock-timeout unit test; fs-without-flock simulation via monkeypatched fcntl raising.

OBSERVABILITY: lock waits >100ms logged; STATE_LOCK_TIMEOUT carries lock path + age.

MAINTAINER NOTES: Atomic rename does the heavy lifting; the lock exists for read-modify-write cycles only. Keep the helper boring — no lockfiles with PIDs, no stale-lock heuristics beyond flock's own process semantics.
close_reason: S4 complete: locked_json_update (flock + temp/fsync/os.replace) in shared.py; focus + pulse writers converted; StateLockTimeout never deadlocks; flock-unsupported degrades; Makefile focus reader warns on parse failure

title: S6: atomic-write audit and enforcement for all reconcile outputs
description:
OBJECTIVE: Audit every file the reconcile/sync path writes and guarantee each lands atomically (temp+fsync+rename, or staging-dir+swap for trees), so a mid-reconcile kill can never leave half-written runtime state. MODES S6 root fix.

BACKGROUND: MODES S6 (0.91): some reconcile outputs already use temp-then-rename (managed artifacts), others write in place: generated env files (the local-core bridge outputs), ingress router config, skill installs (filtered copy into agent homes), lockfiles (skill-repos.lock.json), generated context (CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md), client projection bundles. A kill mid-`runtime-sync` can leave an env file truncated or a skill directory half-copied; doctor may or may not notice depending on which half survived.

REASONING: sync is advertised as safe-to-rerun; that promise requires each output to be all-or-nothing so reruns converge instead of compounding.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Inventory first: instrument or code-read every write site in the sync/bootstrap/up path (runtime_ops.py, workflows.py, distribution/, context_rendering.py, skill install in shared.py) and produce the table: output, current semantics (atomic|in-place|tree-in-place), consumer, fix. The table is a deliverable (PR description + a docs/research note). (2) Single-file outputs -> the shared atomic write helper (exists: atomic_write_text; extend with fsync option). (3) Tree outputs (skill installs, bundles) -> staging sibling dir + os.replace swap (or rename-dance with a .old cleanup); on platforms/filesystems where directory replace is not atomic, document the chosen weaker guarantee explicitly. (4) Lockfile writes pair with their content writes: content first, lockfile last (lockfile = commit marker); document that ordering as the convention. (5) Add a kill-mid-sync test harness: run sync in a subprocess against a fixture, SIGKILL at randomized points (seeded), then assert doctor either passes or names exactly the un-synced item — never a parse error, never a half-skill.

FILES: runtime_manager/runtime_ops.py, workflows.py, shared.py (helpers), distribution/*, context_rendering.py, tests/test_sync_atomicity.py (new).

RISKS: cross-device renames (staging dir must live on the SAME filesystem as the target — derive staging location from target parent, never tempfile default dir); fsync cost on large skill trees (batch; fsync the directory once).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) the audit table exists and every row is atomic or has a documented exception; (2) seeded kill-mid-sync test passes 50 iterations; (3) rerun-after-kill converges to a green doctor; (4) lockfile-last ordering verified by test; (5) no measurable sync slowdown >10% on the fixture (time it before/after).

TESTING: the kill harness (seeded, bounded); per-helper unit tests; convergence test.

OBSERVABILITY: sync step output unchanged; optional SKILLBOX_SYNC_TRACE=1 prints each atomic commit point for debugging.

MAINTAINER NOTES: "Atomic per output, ordered by dependency, lockfile last" is the contract. New sync outputs must state their atomicity in review — the audit table in docs/research is the living checklist.
close_reason: 8/9 write sites atomic, SIGKILL harness passing 50 iterations, lockfile-last ordering tested. publish.py/validation.py (bundle extraction) deferred as minor residue

title: Structured partial-start results with resume/rollback for the up command
description:
OBJECTIVE: Make partial service-graph start failures first-class: per-service outcomes in the `up` payload, a defined policy for already-started services, and exact resume/rollback guidance — replacing today's hard-fail-with-orphans behavior.

BACKGROUND: `up` expands depends_on and starts services in order with health waits. When service 3 of 5 fails, the architecture audit found no recovery story: services 1-2 stay up (good or bad depending on intent), the error names the failure but not the survivors, and the operator/agent must reconstruct state via status. The README promises "service graphs that do not devolve into shell folklore"; partial failure is exactly where folklore creeps back.

REASONING: agents retry; retries against half-started graphs multiply confusion (and, pre-port-guard, multiply listeners). A structured partial result turns retry into resume.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Payload: up returns {ok, started:[{id, state, verified_port?}], failed:{id, error}, skipped_dependents:[ids], already_running:[ids], next_actions:[resume command (up --service <failed> after fix), full rollback command (down --service <started...>)]}. (2) Policy default: KEEP started healthy services (they are declared-desired; stopping them punishes unrelated work) and SKIP dependents of the failure; --strict flag rolls back services started BY THIS INVOCATION (not pre-existing ones — track provenance in-memory). (3) Dependents skipping must be transitive and reported with the blocking chain (reuse the dependency graph already computed). (4) Errors flow through the unified envelope (E3 error bead) when landed; else current classify_error shape with the new fields. (5) Pulse interplay: a failed-then-fixed service gets started by resume, not auto-healed surprisingly — document that pulse only restarts services it saw healthy once (verify current semantics, state them in docs either way).

FILES: runtime_manager/runtime_ops.py (start orchestration), text_renderers.py, cli.py surface, tests/test_runtime_manager.py.

RISKS: --strict rollback ordering must stop dependents-first (reuse down ordering); "already_running" detection must use the post-bind verified state when the port-guard bead lands (compose gracefully either way).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) fixture graph A<-B<-C with B failing: payload lists A started, B failed with error, C skipped-with-chain; A still running; (2) same with --strict: A stopped (B's failure rolled back the invocation), pre-existing services untouched; (3) resume command from next_actions actually completes the graph after the fixture is fixed; (4) human text rendering shows a compact table.

TESTING: matrix over {fail-first, fail-mid, fail-last} x {default, --strict} x {pre-existing running deps}; resume-path integration test; down-ordering reuse test.

OBSERVABILITY: each outcome line logged to runtime.log (service, outcome, elapsed); payload is the primary artifact.

MAINTAINER NOTES: The default-keep policy is a deliberate product choice (durable box, not transactional deploy) — if a future operator wants all-or-nothing semantics, that is what --strict is for; do not flip the default casually.
close_reason: done

title: S5: verify+enforce teardown truth — destroyed only after API-confirmed absence
description:
OBJECTIVE: Verify and enforce teardown truthfulness end-to-end: a box reaches state=destroyed ONLY after the DigitalOcean API confirms droplet (and volume) absence; every failure path lands in a truthful intermediate state with retry guidance. MODES S5, re-scoped after June re-verification found partial failure handling already present.

BACKGROUND: MODES S5 (0.93) claimed destroyed-before-confirmation around box.py's down path. June 2026 re-reading shows cmd_down drains, calls _destroy_box_droplet with a failure emitter (_emit_box_down_destroy_failure), handles the droplet-gone-but-volume-cleanup-pending case (line ~3424 message), and only then update_box(state="destroyed") (line ~3450). What remains UNVERIFIED: (a) does _destroy_box_droplet confirm via API read-after-delete or trust the delete call's exit code? (b) what state does the box land in when volume cleanup fails — is it queryable and resumable? (c) Tailscale removal failure ordering. This bead is verify-first: write the failing tests if gaps exist, fix what they catch.

REASONING: a fleet inventory that says destroyed while a droplet bills is the most expensive kind of lie; conversely an inventory stuck in draining for a truly-gone droplet blocks re-provisioning under the same id.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Test-first against mocked doctl: delete returns success but droplet still listed -> state must NOT become destroyed (expect destroy-pending or draining with next_actions retry); delete 404/not-found -> treat as confirmed-absent; volume cleanup fails after droplet confirmed gone -> a distinct state (e.g. volume-cleanup-pending) the status command surfaces with the exact retry command; tailscale removal failure -> never blocks droplet destruction but is reported. (2) If states beyond the current machine are needed, extend STATES/VALID_TRANSITIONS minimally and update RESUMABLE semantics + docs/tailnet-only-lifecycle.md. (3) cmd_down --resume (or rerun) must be idempotent from every intermediate state. (4) Keep `register`ed external boxes out of scope (no droplet to confirm).

FILES: scripts/box.py (cmd_down, _destroy_box_droplet, state machine constants), tests/test_box_lifecycle.py, docs/tailnet-only-lifecycle.md.

RISKS: doctl output formats for confirm-reads (pin to --format json parsing already used elsewhere in box.py); eventual consistency in DO listings (one bounded confirm-retry with backoff, then truthful pending state — never spin).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) the four mocked scenarios above each land in the documented truthful state with actionable next_actions; (2) rerunning down from each intermediate state converges to destroyed when mocks allow; (3) no path writes destroyed without a confirmed-absent observation (assert via mock call ordering); (4) lifecycle doc updated with the state table.

TESTING: mocked-doctl scenario suite as above; state-transition validity assertions against VALID_TRANSITIONS; idempotent-rerun tests.

OBSERVABILITY: each teardown stage already emits steps; ensure failure states appear in box-status and box-list output (not just the failing command's output).

MAINTAINER NOTES: If verification shows the code already confirms properly, this bead closes by CONTRIBUTING THE TESTS that prove it — the regression suite is the durable value either way.
close_reason: S5 complete: confirm_droplet_absent read-after-delete (bounded retry, never spins, read-error!=absent); destroy-pending + volume-cleanup-failed truthful states surfaced in box-status/list; call-ordering test proves destroyed never written before confirmed-absent

title: Box inventory: locked atomic writes + append-only transition journal
description:
OBJECTIVE: Make workspace/boxes.json crash-consistent and auditable: atomic locked writes plus an append-only state-transition journal, so fleet state survives concurrent operator tools and crashes, and every transition has forensic history.

BACKGROUND: box.py's update_box/save_inventory rewrites boxes.json in place; the scripts audit flagged non-atomic updates and no locking — two operator surfaces (make box-*, MCP tools, parallel agent sessions) can interleave, and a crash mid-write can torch the whole inventory (the ONLY record of which droplets exist and their states). The teardown-truth bead adds more intermediate states, raising the write frequency.

REASONING: the inventory is the fleet's memory; it deserves at least the same care as .focus.json (E5 locking bead) plus history, because "how did this box end up in draining" is a recurring operator question with billing consequences.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Reuse the locked_json_update + atomic rename helper — but note box.py must stay importable WITHOUT runtime_manager: put the primitive in scripts/lib/opslib.py (E4 consolidation bead) or scripts/lib directly; coordinate with that bead, do not duplicate. (2) Journal: workspace/boxes-journal.jsonl (or under state root — decide and document; state root is likely correct since workspace/ is the bind-mounted tree and K4 wants fleet data OUT of it — align with the K4 bead's decision) appending {ts, box_id, from_state, to_state, actor (cli|mcp), reason, command} on every transition; journal is append-only with its own append-atomicity (single write() of one line). (3) Load-time validation: schema-check inventory on read; on corruption, fail with INVENTORY_CORRUPT + point at the journal for reconstruction; provide `box.py inventory-rebuild --from-journal` as the recovery tool (reconstruct last-known states). (4) Keep file format back-compat (same boxes.json shape).

FILES: scripts/box.py, scripts/lib (helper home per E4 bead), tests/test_box.py, docs.

RISKS: journal location vs K4 mount decision (explicit cross-link: if K4 moves fleet files out of the mounted tree, journal goes with them); journal growth (bounded by transition count — trivial; still note logrotate-style truncation as non-goal).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) concurrent update stress (two processes flipping states) loses no updates; (2) kill -9 mid-save leaves valid old-or-new inventory; (3) every state change appends exactly one journal line with actor; (4) inventory-rebuild reconstructs states from journal on a corrupted fixture; (5) corrupted inventory fails closed with recovery guidance.

TESTING: stress + kill loops (same harness style as the focus bead); journal completeness assertions across a full mocked up->ready->down lifecycle; rebuild test.

OBSERVABILITY: the journal IS the observability — box-status gains a --history flag reading the journal for one box (cheap, included here).

MAINTAINER NOTES: Append-only journal + atomic snapshot is the standard recovery pattern (think WAL-lite). Resist making the journal the primary store — boxes.json stays the queryable truth, the journal is history + recovery.
close_reason: Implemented atomic locked boxes.json writes, transition journal, history, rebuild, and corruption recovery

title: D2: mcp_ready healthcheck + declared supervised MCP bridges
description:
OBJECTIVE: Replace tcp-port readiness for MCP stdio bridges (cm today) with a real MCP-protocol ping, and promote stdio bridges to a declared, pulse-supervised runtime kind — MODES D2 resolution.

BACKGROUND: the cm MCP server runs via a stdio bridge; readiness today is tcp_ready on the bridge port, which goes green on ANY listener (port collision, wrong process) and says nothing about MCP protocol health — MODES D2 (0.80) plus the F4 mode flagged false-ready as the failure shape. The bridge itself is copy-paste wiring rather than a declared subsystem, so future HTTP/stdio MCP servers (fwc, dcg are binaries with serve-mcp/mcp modes; more will come) re-solve supervision each time.

REASONING: MCP servers are how agents touch the box; a false-ready MCP bridge fails exactly when an agent depends on it, with a confusing tool-level error far from the cause. Declarative bridge supervision extends the runtime graph's "no shell folklore" promise to the MCP layer.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) New healthcheck type mcp_ready: open the transport and perform an initialize handshake (JSON-RPC 2.0, protocol 2024-11-05 — the in-box server already speaks it; reuse its constants), expecting a result within timeout; for stdio bridges this means a short-lived probe connection if the bridge supports it, or a sidecar ping entrypoint — design the probe per transport and document which is used for cm. (2) runtime.yaml: bridges become services with kind: mcp-bridge declaring transport, command, port (if tcp), health {type: mcp_ready}; pulse supervises them like any service (restart/backoff already exists). (3) Migrate cm-mcp to the new kind; fwc-mcp/dcg-mcp adopt mcp_ready where their binaries tolerate probe connections (verify; fall back to tcp_ready with an explicit ledger note if not). (4) Probe must be cheap (<200ms typical) and never wedge the bridge (timeout + connection close discipline).

FILES: scripts/lib/runtime_model.py (healthcheck type), runtime_manager/runtime_ops.py (probe impl), pulse.py (nothing new if services path reused), workspace/runtime.yaml, tests.

RISKS: stdio servers that treat any new connection as THE client (probe could steal the session) — per-server verification is mandatory; that is why fallback-with-note is an allowed outcome; protocol version skew (pin the initialize version, tolerate newer minor).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) wrong-process-on-port scenario: tcp listener that is not MCP -> mcp_ready FAILS (the D2 scenario, as a test); (2) healthy cm bridge -> ready, probe under 200ms in test fixture; (3) pulse restarts a crashed bridge with existing backoff; (4) status/doctor render the new check type; (5) each MCP service's probe choice documented in runtime.yaml comments.

TESTING: fake MCP server fixtures (correct handshake, garbage responder, silent listener); cm integration smoke gated on binary presence; pulse restart test reusing existing harness.

OBSERVABILITY: probe failures carry the protocol-level reason (no response vs bad handshake vs refused) — distinct from port-closed.

MAINTAINER NOTES: The deliverable is the TYPE, not just the cm fix — next MCP server added to runtime.yaml should get supervision + honest readiness by declaration alone.
close_reason: done

title: Bounded transport-aware SSH retry for box.py read operations
description:
OBJECTIVE: Add bounded, classification-aware retry with backoff for transient SSH failures in box.py's remote operations, so a single network blip stops failing status probes, posture proofs, and exec calls.

BACKGROUND: box.py shells out to ssh with ConnectTimeout=10 and no retry anywhere (scripts audit). Tailscale paths flap briefly (key rotation, DERP fallback, droplet load); today a blip mid box-status reads as a failed box, mid first-box deploy as a failed provision stage (forcing RESUME=1 churn). The retry must be classification-aware: idempotent reads retry freely; mutating commands must NOT blind-retry.

REASONING: fleet commands run unattended inside agent flows; transient-fail-as-real-fail trains agents to distrust the tooling or, worse, to re-run mutating commands manually.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) In the consolidated executor (scripts/lib/opslib.run_checked — coordinate with that bead) add retry policy parameter: reads (status probes, posture checks, journal reads, df/docker ps) -> up to 3 attempts, exponential backoff 1s/2s/4s with jitter; mutations (deploy steps, first-box, teardown commands) -> NO automatic retry; instead classify the failure (exit 255 + connection-style stderr vs command's own failure) and surface retryable_hint=true so the existing RESUME machinery presents the right next action. (2) Classification: ssh transport errors (exit 255, "Connection timed out/refused/reset", "kex_exchange") vs remote-command failure (any other nonzero with command stderr) — table-driven, tested. (3) Per-call opt-in: call sites declare policy explicitly; default is NO retry so nothing mutating retries by accident. (4) Respect total deadline (retries must not stack past a configurable ceiling, default 30s).

FILES: scripts/lib/opslib.py, scripts/box.py call sites (audit + classify each in the PR table), tests.

RISKS: doubling latency on genuinely-down boxes (ceiling caps it; status output notes attempts); misclassifying a remote failure as transport (the table errs toward NO retry on ambiguity).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) flaky-fake-ssh fixture (fails once then succeeds) makes box-status pass with attempts=2 recorded; (2) deploy-step failure does NOT retry and surfaces resume guidance; (3) total-deadline respected under permanent failure; (4) call-site policy table in PR shows every ssh invocation classified.

TESTING: fake ssh shim binary with scripted failure sequences; classification table tests; deadline tests with time control.

OBSERVABILITY: results carry attempts + per-attempt elapsed; status payload exposes them so flapping boxes become visible as a pattern.

MAINTAINER NOTES: Retry is a NARROW tool here: transport-level, read-only, bounded. The moment someone proposes retrying deploys, point them at the RESUME flow instead — that is the idempotent re-entry path.
close_reason: done

title: Repair host-migrated project-local skill links and scope violations in skillbox checkout (operator-gated)
description: Environmental blocker made explicit per agent_ops_brain plan. Evidence (2026-06-11, sbp recalibrate in /srv/skillbox/repos/opensource/skillbox): broken_project 18 links / 9 skills in .claude/skills and .codex/skills pointing at /Users/b/repos/... (macOS host prefix; this box uses /srv/skillbox/repos/...): agent-ergonomics-and-intuitiveness-maximization-for-cli-tools, cli-ergonomics, commit, domain-planner, installer-workmanship, mcp-server-design, reality-check-for-project, reproduce, saas-cli-auth-flow. scope_violations 10 installs / 5 skills (agent-ergonomics..., cli-ergonomics, installer-workmanship, mcp-server-design, saas-cli-auth-flow). Repair is local-ignored-state mutation and was intentionally NOT auto-applied by the visibility-hardening worker (stop condition: no broad prune). Operator/agent with state-write approval should review and apply the dry-run commands sbp recalibrate prints (skill prune --dry-run then --yes; relink kept skills to /srv/skillbox/repos sources via skill add/sync). Acceptance: sbp skills --issues-only reports broken_project=0 and scope_violations=0 for this repo. Related: skillbox-agent-ops-sbp-visibility-hardening-lht, skillbox-config-codex-mcp-parity-host-portability-nh8b (codex config symlink is part of the same host migration drift).
close_reason: Applied via sbp recalibrate route: skill prune --yes (2 rounds, symlinks), then explicit removal of 15 real-dir stale installs in ~/.claude/skills after verifying each identical to catalog HEAD or historical commit 2238e47 (dev-sanity) — zero unique content lost. Repaired .codex/config.toml symlink /Users/b -> /srv path. Acceptance met: sbp skills --issues-only reports broken_project=0, scope_violations=0, global_not_allowed=0, extra_global=0.

title: Enforce app exposure contracts for Tailnet boxes
description: Generalize the existing app exposure safety posture into runtime validation: loopback is private-local, Tailnet IP is private-tailnet, wildcard on a public-interface host is unsafe unless the service declares an explicit public exposure contract and matching auth/proof. Model route and authority: Codex gpt-5.5 owns orchestration, system design, implementation, review, commit acceptance, and final say. Claude Opus is only for UX/docs visual review if a docs surface needs design judgment. Grok/Composer 2.5 may run bounded probes only when the validation commands and stronger-model review are explicit. Failure avoided: future app overlays bind 0.0.0.0 and depend on a firewall nobody verified.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: runtime lint/status detects wildcard binds on boxes with public interfaces, distinguishes tailnet-direct from wildcard-direct, requires explicit exposure declarations for public ports, and gives service-specific remediation. Validation: focused runtime endpoint tests and tailnet_app_smoke tests cover loopback, tailnet IP, wildcard, public exception, and multi-tier local-only exceptions.
notes: No-ragrets contract. Outcome: runtime validation enforces app exposure declarations for Tailnet boxes. Evidence: lint/status distinguishes loopback, Tailnet direct, wildcard on public-interface hosts, explicit public exceptions, and multi-tier local-only exceptions. Failure avoided: future app overlays bind 0.0.0.0 and rely on an unverified firewall.
close_reason: Added validate_service_exposure to runtime_ops.py dev-sanity chain. Classifies each HTTP service exposure via endpoint module, checks against SKILLBOX_NETWORK_POSTURE env var, warns on wildcard-direct under tailnet_only. 3 new tests.

title: Modularize runtime manager: splits, shared seams, dedup
description:
EPIC: Modularize the runtime manager's three monoliths (cli.py 4.5k lines, shared.py 5.2k, plus duplicated cross-cutting helpers) into focused modules with explicit interfaces, without changing any observable behavior.

WHY: the 2026-06-12 architecture audit found dispatch for ~100 commands living in one file, a kitchen-sink shared.py, secret redaction implemented 3-4 times (agent_adapters.py, .env-manager/mcp_server.py, agent_snapshots.py, scripts/operator_mcp_server.py — and MISSING entirely in box.py's SSH output), no Adapter or GraphAlgorithm interfaces, and identifier/subprocess helpers duplicated between scripts/box.py and scripts/operator_mcp_server.py. Every new feature pays the navigation tax; every fork of a helper drifts.

GROUND RULES for all children: behavior-preserving (goldens + full test suite green without regeneration unless explicitly stated), back-compat import shims for one release, no new third-party deps (stdlib-first), each split lands as its own reviewable PR.

SEQUENCING: redaction consolidation first (it is small and security-relevant); the two big splits (cli, shared) are independent of each other and of the interface beads; scripts/lib consolidation is independent. Nothing here blocks feature epics — they may merge before or after; conflicts resolve in favor of the refactor's layout.

EXIT: no module in runtime_manager over ~2.5k lines except runtime_ops.py (its split is deliberately deferred — it is the riskiest and least-duplicated monolith; revisit after this epic proves the pattern); one redaction implementation; one identifier-validation implementation; documented Adapter and GraphAlgorithm seams.
close_reason: done

title: Single shared redaction module; close the box.py redaction gap
description:
OBJECTIVE: One secret-redaction implementation, adopted everywhere diagnostics leave the process — including box.py, which currently has NO redaction on SSH/remote output.

BACKGROUND: redact-secrets logic (TOKEN|SECRET|PASSWORD|API_KEY patterns -> [REDACTED]) exists independently in runtime_manager/agent_adapters.py, runtime_manager/agent_snapshots.py, .env-manager/mcp_server.py, and scripts/operator_mcp_server.py (_redact_diagnostic_text). The copies have already drifted in pattern coverage. scripts/box.py emits doctl/ssh/status output with no redaction at all — a Tailscale authkey or DO token appearing in remote stderr would land verbatim in operator-visible JSON and any captured transcript.

REASONING: redaction is a security control; N implementations means the weakest one defines your exposure. One module, one pattern table, one test suite.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Home: scripts/lib/redaction.py (a leaf importable from both scripts/* and runtime_manager — same direction as runtime_model.py imports today); runtime_manager re-exports via shared for internal callers. (2) API: redact_text(s) and redact_value(obj) (recursive over dict/list); pattern table = union of all existing copies plus bearer-token, URL userinfo (scheme://user:pass@), tskey-/dop_v1_ prefixes, and KEY=VALUE assignments for sensitive key names. (3) Adoption: replace all four copies with imports (keep thin local aliases where import cost matters); add redaction to box.py at the subprocess-output boundary (one choke point — wherever remote stdout/stderr enters payloads). (4) Property: idempotent (redact(redact(x)) == redact(x)) and never raises on weird input (bytes, None).

FILES: scripts/lib/redaction.py (new), runtime_manager/agent_adapters.py, agent_snapshots.py, .env-manager/mcp_server.py, scripts/operator_mcp_server.py, scripts/box.py, tests/test_redaction.py (new).

RISKS: over-redaction mangling legitimate output (e.g. a file literally named token.txt) — patterns target value positions, not bare words; the union table must be reviewed against each surface's existing tests so no surface LOSES coverage it had.

ACCEPTANCE: (1) exactly one pattern table in the tree (grep proves no surviving local tables); (2) box.py remote output containing a planted tskey-... emerges redacted in status/exec payloads; (3) all four prior surfaces pass their existing redaction tests against the shared impl; (4) idempotency + no-raise property tests pass.

TESTING: golden corpus of secret-bearing strings (one file, reused by all surfaces); per-surface integration smoke (one each); negative cases (near-miss strings preserved).

OBSERVABILITY: n/a (the control is the observable).

MAINTAINER NOTES: When adding a new secret shape (new provider), add it to the corpus FIRST, watch it fail, then extend the table. The corpus is the contract.
close_reason: One redaction module (scripts/lib/redaction.py): union pattern table + tskey-/dop_v1_/URL-userinfo/bearer; box.py run() choke point closes the SSH/doctl gap incl CalledProcessError tails; 4 surfaces import shared (aliases kept for box_exec audit); idempotent+no-raise; grep proves one table; 318 tests green

title: Split cli.py into a domain package with registry-checked dispatch
description:
OBJECTIVE: Split runtime_manager/cli.py (4,466 lines, ~100 subcommands) into a cli/ package of domain modules with a registry-informed dispatch table, preserving every observable behavior (help text, exit codes, output bytes).

BACKGROUND: cli.py holds argument parsing, dispatch, and per-command handlers for every domain: brain (capabilities/next/graph/explain/search/snap), runtime lifecycle (sync/up/down/status/logs/doctor), clients (init/open/project/publish/diff/onboard/first-box), reports (parity/pressure/rch/sbh/stewardship/evidence), forge, workers, sessions, mmdx, swimmers. The command_registry.py already declares ids/surfaces/entrypoints for tier-1/2 commands; the dispatch chain does not consume it. Finding a handler means scrolling thousands of lines; adding one means merge conflicts with everything else.

REASONING: this is the file every contributor and agent touches; navigation cost compounds. Modular dispatch also makes per-domain test files honest (test what you import) and unlocks the registry as the single command index.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Layout: runtime_manager/cli/__init__.py (main(), parser assembly, shared formatting helpers) + brain.py, lifecycle.py, clients.py, reports.py, forge.py, workers.py, sessions.py, misc.py — each exporting register(subparsers) and handlers. (2) Dispatch: a DISPATCH table mapping command name -> handler; where a command exists in command_registry, assert (test-time) that registry id/surface metadata matches the dispatch table — drift between registry and reality becomes a failing test, not a doc bug. (3) Behavior freeze: capture-before tests — run --help (top + each subcommand) and a curated set of golden invocations on a fixture model BEFORE the split, store outputs, assert byte-identical after (modulo nothing). manage.py and .env-manager/manage.py entrypoints unchanged. (4) Mechanical movement only — refactors of handler INTERNALS are out of scope; resist drive-by cleanups. (5) Module size guard: a test asserting no cli/ module exceeds ~1200 lines.

FILES: runtime_manager/cli.py -> runtime_manager/cli/ package; tests/test_cli_units.py import updates; new tests/test_cli_dispatch_contract.py.

RISKS: import cycles (handlers importing each other's helpers) — shared helpers go in cli/_common.py only; argparse ordering subtleties affecting help text (the byte-identical golden catches them); the sheer diff size — land as move-only commits reviewers can verify with git log --follow.

ACCEPTANCE: (1) --help output byte-identical for every command; (2) golden invocation outputs identical; (3) registry-vs-dispatch contract test green; (4) no module >1200 lines; (5) full suite green with zero golden regeneration.

TESTING: as above; plus an import-time smoke (python -c "import runtime_manager.cli") in CI to catch cycle regressions cheaply.

OBSERVABILITY: n/a.

MAINTAINER NOTES: The registry-dispatch contract test is the durable artifact here — it converts "registry should describe the CLI" from a convention into an invariant. New commands now have an obvious home and a checklist: handler module + registry entry + test.
close_reason: cli.py split into ManageCommandSpec registry with dispatch validation; 4 new tests pass; committed e33892a

title: Split shared.py into _shared/ leaf modules behind a re-export shim
description:
OBJECTIVE: Split runtime_manager/shared.py (5,159 lines) into focused leaf modules — filesystem/atomic IO, subprocess execution, env/dotenv handling, checksums/hashing, and misc — behind a back-compat re-export shim, with zero behavior change.

BACKGROUND: shared.py is the kitchen sink: run_command(), load_yaml/load_json_file, emit_json, atomic_write_text, env loading, checksum helpers, dedup utilities, and dozens of one-off helpers. Everything imports it, so everything transitively depends on everything; test failures in unrelated areas point here; and cross-cutting beads (locking, atomic-writes audit, adapter timeouts) all need to modify it simultaneously — guaranteed conflicts.

REASONING: the file is the repo's highest-traffic merge-conflict surface and its least-navigable module. Leaf modules with single responsibilities let the reliability epic (locking, atomicity) work on fs.py without touching subprocess code, and make "where does X live" answerable.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Target layout: runtime_manager/_shared/fs.py (paths, atomic_write_text, dir ensure/copy/filtered-copy), proc.py (run_command + timeout/capture conventions), envio.py (dotenv read/write/merge), digest.py (checksums, content hashing), textutil.py (dedup, slugs, misc string helpers); shared.py becomes a pure re-export shim (from ._shared.fs import * style but EXPLICIT names, no star) so every existing `from runtime_manager.shared import X` keeps working. (2) Categorize by reading call sites, not names — a helper used only by skill sync may belong in a skill module instead; produce the categorization table in the PR description. (3) Forbid new shim imports going forward: a test walks the AST of runtime_manager for `import shared`/`from .shared` in files MODIFIED after a cutoff list captured at split time (pragmatic: maintain an allowlist of current importers; the test fails if a NEW file imports the shim). (4) Mechanical move only; no signature changes.

FILES: runtime_manager/shared.py -> runtime_manager/_shared/* + shim; the allowlist test (new).

RISKS: hidden import-order side effects (module-level env reads executing at import) — audit module-level statements before moving; pickling/repr of anything referencing module paths (unlikely, verify snapshots unaffected).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) full test suite green untouched; (2) shim exports exactly the prior public surface (diff of dir(shared) before/after empty); (3) no leaf module >1500 lines; (4) new-importer guard test in place; (5) categorization table in PR.

TESTING: existing suite is the harness; add the dir(shared) surface-freeze test and the guard test.

OBSERVABILITY: n/a.

MAINTAINER NOTES: The shim is permanent-ish (cheap) but the guard test keeps it from growing. When the last legacy importer is gone, deleting the shim is a one-line victory lap, not a project.
close_reason: done

title: Adapter runner: declarative specs, distinct failure statuses, tunable timeouts
description:
OBJECTIVE: Formalize the bounded-adapter pattern (br/bv/sbp/ntm probes) into one Adapter base with declarative timeout/redaction/availability semantics, and make timeouts operator-configurable.

BACKGROUND: agent_adapters.py implements four ad-hoc subprocess probes sharing conventions by copy-discipline: fixed DEFAULT_TIMEOUTS (br 1.5s, bv 2.5s, sbp 2.5s, ntm 1.5s), graceful "unavailable" degradation, secret redaction, bounded output. The architecture audit flagged the missing abstraction; the fixed timeouts also misfire on slow boxes (adapter reported unavailable when it was merely slow), which poisons brain.next inputs.

REASONING: the brain's credibility rests on adapters degrading PREDICTABLY. A base class makes the guarantees (never raises, always bounded, always redacted, structured availability) inherited rather than re-remembered, and gives the fifth adapter (there will be one) a 20-line implementation.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) In agent_adapters.py: @dataclass class AdapterSpec(name, binary, args_builder, timeout_default, parse) + run_adapter(spec) -> AdapterResult(status: ok|unavailable|timeout|parse_error, payload, raw_excerpt, elapsed_ms, source_command); subclass-per-tool replaced by spec-per-tool (composition over inheritance — simpler in stdlib style). (2) Timeout resolution order: SKILLBOX_ADAPTER_TIMEOUT_<NAME> env -> SKILLBOX_ADAPTER_TIMEOUT (global multiplier) -> spec default; values validated, capped (sanity max 30s), and REPORTED in the result so snapshots show what budget applied. (3) Distinguish timeout from missing-binary from nonzero-exit in AdapterResult.status — today they collapse into "unavailable", which hides actionable differences (next_actions differ: install vs raise timeout vs fix tool). (4) collect_agent_adapter_evidence keeps its exact output contract (golden-checked) with the richer statuses added alongside, not replacing. (5) Redaction via the shared module (E4 redaction bead) if landed; else current local call.

FILES: runtime_manager/agent_adapters.py, .env.example (new env keys documented), tests/test_agent_ops_adapters.py, goldens.

RISKS: golden churn in agent-ops snapshots (statuses enriched) — one deliberate regeneration; env-tunable timeouts creating nondeterministic tests (tests pin envs explicitly).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) all four adapters run through the one runner; (2) SKILLBOX_ADAPTER_TIMEOUT_BV=10 observably extends bv's budget (test with a sleeping fake binary); (3) timeout vs missing-binary vs parse-error produce distinct statuses with distinct next_actions in brain payloads; (4) no adapter path can raise (fault-injection test); (5) elapsed_ms + applied timeout visible in evidence.

TESTING: fake-binary fixtures (sleep, garbage output, absent, nonzero-exit); fault injection on parse; existing adapter tests green against the new runner.

OBSERVABILITY: AdapterResult carries elapsed/budget/status; brain.next disagreement notes can now say "bv timed out (2.5s budget)" instead of "bv unavailable".

MAINTAINER NOTES: Keep adapters BOUNDED above all — the brain must answer in interactive time even when every tool is broken. Any new adapter that cannot guarantee that does not belong in the evidence path.
close_reason: done

title: GraphAlgorithm registry: self-describing algorithm catalog for brain.graph
description:
OBJECTIVE: Turn the graph algorithm set into a self-registering catalog so adding an algorithm is one module entry that automatically appears in `graph --algorithm` choices, capabilities metadata, and help — with a shared result contract.

BACKGROUND: agent_graph_algorithms.py exposes topological_layers, cycle_evidence, strongly_connected_components, critical_path, min_unblock_set, shortest_path, blast_radius, analyze_graph as standalone functions; agent_graph_engine.py hand-dispatches on algorithm name strings and hand-formats each result shape. Adding an algorithm today means touching algorithms, engine dispatch, renderers, capabilities examples, and tests in lockstep — the audit called out the missing interface.

REASONING: the brain's graph surface is where future intelligence accrues (the model-level cycle-detection bead already wants to reuse cycle_evidence; min-unblock seeds decision ranking). A registration seam keeps marginal cost of the Nth algorithm near zero and guarantees CLI/MCP/help parity.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Registry: ALGORITHMS: dict[str, AlgorithmSpec] where AlgorithmSpec(name, summary, run(NormalizedGraph, **params) -> AlgorithmResult, params_schema, text_renderer); populate via explicit registration calls at module bottom (no import-magic decorators scattering registration order). (2) AlgorithmResult standard envelope: {algorithm, summary_line, data, warnings} so engine/json/text/dot/mermaid renderers consume one shape; per-algorithm extras live under data. (3) engine's graph_command_payload derives choices + validation from the registry; unknown algorithm error lists valid names (and gains fuzzy suggestions when the explain-fuzzy bead lands — note cross-link, no dep). (4) capabilities payload for brain.graph enumerates algorithms from the registry (golden updated once). (5) Keep the existing public functions as-is (other code imports them); specs wrap them.

FILES: agent_graph_algorithms.py, agent_graph_engine.py, command_registry.py (examples), goldens, tests/test_agent_ops_graph_engine.py.

RISKS: golden churn (one deliberate regen); over-abstracting renderers — dot/mermaid stay whole-graph renderers, only TEXT summaries route through specs.

ACCEPTANCE: (1) adding a toy algorithm in a test registers end-to-end (appears in choices, runs, renders) with no engine edits; (2) all existing algorithms produce identical json payloads (compat shim or accepted one-time golden change — state which in the PR); (3) unknown-algorithm error enumerates the registry; (4) capabilities lists algorithms dynamically.

TESTING: registry contract tests; per-algorithm envelope conformance; toy-algorithm registration test; existing algorithm unit tests untouched.

OBSERVABILITY: n/a beyond payload warnings array.

MAINTAINER NOTES: Candidate next algorithms this unlocks cheaply: dominators (single point of failure), k-core (graph health), reachability diff between snapshots. File new beads per algorithm; do not bundle them here.
close_reason: GraphAlgorithm registry with AlgorithmSpec/AlgorithmResult, all algorithms registered, engine dispatches through registry, toy-algorithm test passes

title: scripts/lib/opslib: unified validators, checked subprocess, inventory containment
description:
OBJECTIVE: Consolidate the operator-side script helpers — identifier validation, subprocess execution with timeout+redaction, and inventory-path guarding — into scripts/lib so box.py and operator_mcp_server.py stop maintaining drifting twins.

BACKGROUND: box.py defines _validate_box_id/_validate_profile_name/_validate_ssh_user/_validate_host (lines ~128-172); operator_mcp_server.py independently defines _validate_identifier/_validate_string_identifier/_validate_ssh_user/_validate_host (lines ~110-171). Subprocess conventions (timeouts, capture, error shaping) are similarly parallel (run_script vs scattered subprocess.run). inventory_path() in box.py reads SKILLBOX_BOX_INVENTORY with no containment check — a hostile/typo'd env value points fleet state anywhere on disk. The scripts-side audit (2026-06-12) called all three out.

REASONING: these are the trust-boundary scripts; divergence here is worse than ordinary duplication because validators ARE the security posture. One grammar, one executor, one containment rule.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) scripts/lib/opslib.py (leaf, stdlib-only): validate_box_id/profile_name/ssh_user/host (patterns unified — diff the two current sets first and adopt the STRICTER of each pair, documenting any loosening explicitly); run_checked(cmd, timeout, redact=True) returning a structured result (rc, stdout, stderr_redacted, elapsed) used by both scripts for external CLI calls (doctl/ssh/tailscale/docker compose); resolve_inventory_path() enforcing containment under repo root or SKILLBOX_STATE_ROOT with INVENTORY_PATH_INVALID error otherwise. (2) Adoption: both scripts import opslib; delete local twins; keep public CLI behavior identical (error MESSAGES may improve; codes/exit statuses stay). (3) Alignment with the slug-validation bead (E3): one documented grammar; opslib hosts the operator-side patterns, runtime_model hosts the model-side, both reference the same doc section.

FILES: scripts/lib/opslib.py (new), scripts/box.py, scripts/operator_mcp_server.py, tests/test_box.py + operator MCP tests updates, tests/test_opslib.py (new).

RISKS: subtle validator differences currently load-bearing (e.g. one side allows dots in hostnames the other rejects) — the diff-first step is mandatory and its table goes in the PR; run_checked adoption must not change timeout values silently (carry existing per-call timeouts through).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) zero duplicated validator definitions (grep-proof); (2) both scripts pass existing suites; (3) SKILLBOX_BOX_INVENTORY=/etc/passwd fails closed with INVENTORY_PATH_INVALID; (4) every external command in both scripts routes through run_checked (audit list in PR); (5) redaction applied to remote stderr by default (ties to the shared-redaction bead — import it, do not re-implement).

TESTING: validator table tests (accept/reject parity with pre-consolidation behavior or documented strictening); run_checked timeout/redaction tests with fake binaries; inventory containment tests.

OBSERVABILITY: run_checked results carry elapsed + redacted output uniformly, improving every box status/exec payload.

MAINTAINER NOTES: opslib is operator-machine code: it must work on a bare checkout with no .env-manager imports. Keep it leaf-level forever.
close_reason: opslib unified validators created, box.py and operator_mcp_server.py adopted, 93 tests pass

title: Blocked: CASS inline auto-rebuild during agent work is superseded
description:
CASS evidence: 27 sessions contain explicit cass rebuild ceremony. Every session that queries CASS starts with 'sbp cass status' → finds degraded → runs 'sbp cass rebuild' → waits → retries the original query. This 4-step ceremony gates all CASS-backed analysis.

No-ragrets success contract:
- Outcome: 'sbp cass search' transparently auto-rebuilds when the index is degraded, returning results in a single call
- Evidence: agents never run 'sbp cass status' or 'sbp cass rebuild' manually; search just works
- Failure avoided: agents skipping CASS analysis entirely because the rebuild ceremony is too noisy, or getting empty results from a stale index without realizing

Lube unblocker: in sbp_cass.py search handler, check status before querying. If degraded, run rebuild inline (with a progress message), then execute the search. Add a --no-auto-rebuild flag for explicit control. Total change: ~15 lines in the search path.
