title: sbp skill promote --suggest — CASS-evidence-driven default promotion with what-if blast radius
description:
Files: .env-manager/runtime_manager/evidence.py + scripts/sbp (sbp evidence CASS join); .skillbox/skill-overrides.yaml (NEW, repo-committed); tests/

AUDIENCE: the operator curating fleet policy. Bar: 'skill wiki is actually invoked in 9/10 frontend repos → promote to a frontend default' in one command, with a simulated blast radius first.

WHAT: `--suggest` reads CASS invocation telemetry across the registry (via `sbp evidence`, contamination-safe), ranks candidate promotions by (repos_used/repos_in_cohort, total_invocations, recency) with minimum-repos + minimum-invocations + recency floors; promotion writes a default into the right layer (override or policy) and prints the what-if delta (which repos newly get it; which already had it via a now-redundant pin). Gate behind the what-if preview + drift check so a promotion can't silently break a pinned repo.

DONE: on fixture_fleet with seeded evidence, --suggest returns a deterministic ranked list; a 1/10 skill never outranks a 9/10 skill; promoting the top suggestion makes those repos' what-if show it as a default (not a pin). TEST: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests. DEFERRED. Depends on the default verb and what-if.

## Acceptance Criteria
- --suggest returns a deterministic ranked list honoring minimum-repos, minimum-invocations, and recency floors; a 1/10-repo skill never outranks a 9/10-repo skill.
- Promoting the top suggestion makes those repos' what-if show the skill as a default (not a pin); promotion is gated behind the what-if preview + drift check. Verify: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests.
acceptance_criteria:
- --suggest returns a deterministic ranked list honoring minimum-repos, minimum-invocations, and recency floors; a 1/10-repo skill never outranks a 9/10-repo skill.

title: sbp skill prune --evidence --stale-days N — evidence-backed dead-pin reaping (advisory, reversible)
description:
Files: .env-manager/runtime_manager/evidence.py + .env-manager/runtime_manager/lifecycle.py (evidence-backed pin reaping); .skillbox/skill-overrides.yaml (NEW, repo-committed); tests/

AUDIENCE: the operator fighting override sprawl. Bar: 'pin legacy-skill in ../api: 0 invocations in 90 days → remove?' with one reversible removal — a signal for what is safe to delete that doesn't exist today.

WHAT: join CASS invocation telemetry to the COMMITTED pins; for each pin/force in scope, look up last-invocation + N-day count; rank ascending; emit removal candidates with confidence; --apply removes as a journaled tx (reversible). NEVER auto-remove a policy-REQUIRED skill (only redundant/never-used pins); 'unknown' (a repo with no telemetry) reads as 'don't suggest', not 'unused'. Advisory + reversible, never auto-apply; honor a pin tag keep:always.

DONE: with seeded evidence (pin A used yesterday, pin B unused 120d), --evidence --stale-days 90 lists B not A; --apply removes B reversibly; a policy-required skill is never listed even at 0 invocations. TEST: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests. DEFERRED. Depends on the tx journal and the default verb.

## Acceptance Criteria
- With seeded evidence (pin A used yesterday, pin B unused 120d), --evidence --stale-days 90 lists B and not A; --apply removes B as a reversible tx.
- A policy-required skill is never listed even at 0 invocations; a repo with no telemetry reads as 'don't suggest'; nothing auto-applies. Verify: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests.
acceptance_criteria:
- With seeded evidence (pin A used yesterday, pin B unused 120d), --evidence --stale-days 90 lists B and not A; --apply removes B as a reversible tx.

title: sbp skill recipe export/apply/verify — portable, content-hashed skill-set presets
description:

AUDIENCE: a team/vendor distributing a curated skill posture ('the frontend recipe', 'the security-audit recipe'). Bar: export a signed/hashed bundle and apply it on any machine with a what-if preview and a reversible apply.

title: Precedence spine — fold the override layer into the one canonical reader (collect_skill_visibility) with a total, tested order
description:
Files: .env-manager/runtime_manager/audit_report.py (collect_skill_visibility:645 + explain_skill_visibility:1635 — the ONE merge); .env-manager/runtime_manager/policy_eval.py; .env-manager/runtime_manager/_skill_common.py (LAYER_* ranks); tests/test_skill_overrides.py

AUDIENCE: AI agents reasoning about 'why is X on/off here'. Bar: a single ordered, documented merge that EVERY reader (effective-set AND explain) consults identically — least surprise, total order, no ties.

WHAT: insert the override layer into the ONE function that already is the spine — collect_skill_visibility (.env-manager/runtime_manager/audit_report.py:645) — and route explain_skill_visibility (:1635) through the SAME merge. Define the total order, highest wins: (1) this-invocation CLI flags / --cwd, (2) env (SKILLBOX_OVERLAYS), (3) repo-override-file, (4) operator-state-dir (~/.skillbox-state/overlays), (5) base policy (external skill-scope.yaml; preserve the existing LAYER_ALWAYS_GLOBAL > LAYER_REPO_LOCAL > LAYER_ON_DEMAND sub-order). The global FLOOR (DISPATCHER_CORE = smart, sbp) is a POST-FILTER VETO on pin_off, NOT a winning layer. Committed file outranks operator-state-dir because committed+reviewed+portable beats machine-local+unreviewed. Ephemeral CLI/env win only for THIS invocation and are reported as ephemeral; they do not rewrite durable effective state.

WHY: the override file is meaningless until its relationship to env, state-dir, base, and floor is defined and tested. Single merge path is non-negotiable: the moment a second reader reimplements precedence, `why` lies.

DONE: adjacent-pair precedence tests (each higher layer wins for the same skill); floor vetoes a repo-file pin_off of sbp; for any skill exactly one winning_layer is reported, asserted by LAYER NAME not path string. TEST: tests/test_skill_overrides.py; python3 -m unittest discover -s tests. Depends on the schema reader.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Adjacent-pair precedence tests pass: CLI > env > repo-override-file > operator-state-dir > base-policy; the global floor vetoes a repo-file pin_off of sbp.
- Exactly one winning_layer is reported per skill, asserted by LAYER NAME (not path); the effective-set computation and explain_skill_visibility call the SAME merge function.
- Verify: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests.
acceptance_criteria:
- Adjacent-pair precedence tests pass: CLI > env > repo-override-file > operator-state-dir > base-policy; the global floor vetoes a repo-file pin_off of sbp.
- Exactly one winning_layer is reported per skill, asserted by LAYER NAME (not path); the effective-set computation and explain_skill_visibility call the SAME merge function.
- Verify: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests.

title: Crash-safe, concurrency-safe override writes (flock + fsync + parent-dir fsync; lag-tolerant read-after-write)
description:
Files: .env-manager/runtime_manager/shared.py (atomic_write_text vs atomic_write_json — add fsync+flock); .env-manager/runtime_manager/policy_eval.py (override writer); tests/test_skill_overrides.py

AUDIENCE: operator + AI agents in multi-pane swarms. Bar: a crash or a two-pane race never leaves a torn or lost-update override file — the new source of truth is as durable as the pulse/focus JSON path.

WHAT: route override writes through a writer that does temp-write + fsync(file) + os.replace + fsync(parent dir) under an flock, matching atomic_write_json (the text path atomic_write_text currently does NOT fsync). Add a lag-tolerant read-after-write (re-stat or read through the same fd) per the documented pane-visibility-lag memory. Provide a --no-fsync escape hatch for bulk scripted seeding.

WHY: a torn write to the override file silently changes everyone's effective skill set; concurrent `sbp skill on A` / `on B` from two panes must not lose-update.

DONE: two sequential writes (pin_on:A then pin_on:B) → file contains BOTH; simulated crash between write and replace → file is fully old or fully new, never partial; writer fsyncs file + parent dir. TEST: tests/test_skill_overrides.py (reuse the atomic_write_json harness); python3 -m unittest discover -s tests.

## Acceptance Criteria
- pin_on:A then pin_on:B leaves a file containing BOTH (no lost update; flock serializes concurrent writers).
- A simulated crash between write and replace leaves the file fully old or fully new, never partial; the writer fsyncs the file and the parent dir.
- Verify: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests (reusing the atomic_write_json harness).
acceptance_criteria:
- pin_on:A then pin_on:B leaves a file containing BOTH (no lost update; flock serializes concurrent writers).
- A simulated crash between write and replace leaves the file fully old or fully new, never partial; the writer fsyncs the file and the parent dir.
- Verify: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests (reusing the atomic_write_json harness).
close_reason: Implemented a locked crash-safe repo override writer with sidecar flock, temp-write/rename, file+parent fsync, and read-after-write confirmation. Added sequential, concurrent-process, crash, fsync, and timeout tests. Verified: python3 -m unittest tests.test_skill_overrides tests.test_state_fs tests.test_skill_visibility (58 tests OK); python3 -m unittest discover -s tests (1718 tests OK, skipped=4).

title: Guarantee .skillbox/ is git-tracked while .skillbox-state/ stays ignored
description:

AUDIENCE: operator (the 'committed, survives recalibrate' promise) + AI agents. Bar: the override file is actually committed, not silently swallowed by a glob.

title: Prune firewall — one pin predicate so prune can NEVER unlink a pinned/opted skill
description:
Files: .env-manager/runtime_manager/lifecycle.py (_plan_skill_prune_actions:589, _unlink_skill_action:407 — add pin guard at ~604); tests/test_overlay_regression.py (plan==apply byte-equal)

AUDIENCE: operator (primary) + AI agents. Bar: I can pin a skill and `sbp skill prune --yes` will NEVER touch it — guaranteed by construction, not by remembering a flag. This is the clobber root-cause fix and the highest-leverage, lowest-risk bead in the epic (~10 lines).

BACKGROUND (verified): _plan_skill_prune_actions (.env-manager/runtime_manager/lifecycle.py:589) iterates issue_keys (scope_violations, global_not_allowed, extra_global, broken_global, broken_project; line 596) and at line 604 appends _unlink_skill_action(item) with NO predicate consulting user intent. A manual pin is, by definition, exactly one of those 'violations' against external policy — so today it would be written, look authoritative, then get silently clobbered by the next prune.

WHAT: at line ~603, before appending the unlink, check the merged override set — if the candidate is pin_on / opt-in (or otherwise protected by the precedence spine), CONTINUE and record it in a skipped: [{name, reason: "pinned"}] summary instead of unlinking. A pin_off / opt_out_global is, conversely, deterministically ADDED to the unlink list. Enforce at PLAN-CONSTRUCTION time so the proven dry-run plan == apply plan contract automatically honors the firewall.

WHY: every other idea (idempotent toggle, why, heal) presupposes a pin survives recalibration. DONE: with a pin on X, prune plan omits X's unlink in BOTH dry-run and apply, byte-equal, and reports X under skipped with reason pinned; a pin_off appears in the unlink list. TEST: extend tests/test_overlay_regression.py (the plan==apply byte-equal harness); python3 -m unittest discover -s tests. Depends on the precedence spine.

## Acceptance Criteria
- With a pin on X, the prune plan omits X's unlink in BOTH dry-run and apply (byte-equal) and reports X under skipped:[{name:X,reason:pinned}]; a pin_off appears in the unlink list.
- The pin predicate is enforced at plan-construction in _plan_skill_prune_actions (lifecycle.py ~604), so the proven dry-run==apply contract carries it automatically.
- Verify: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests (extends tests/test_overlay_regression.py).
acceptance_criteria:
- With a pin on X, the prune plan omits X's unlink in BOTH dry-run and apply (byte-equal) and reports X under skipped:[{name:X,reason:pinned}]; a pin_off appears in the unlink list.
- The pin predicate is enforced at plan-construction in _plan_skill_prune_actions (lifecycle.py ~604), so the proven dry-run==apply contract carries it automatically.
- Verify: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests (extends tests/test_overlay_regression.py).

title: skill-repo-set install omits _shared, breaking domain-planner helpers in managed codex home
description: Evidence (2026-06-11): workspace/skill-repos.yaml default-skills installs domain-planner (and other build000r/skills skills that document ../_shared and ~/.claude/skills/_shared dependencies) as filtered copies into ${SKILLBOX_HOME_ROOT}/.claude/skills and .codex/skills, but _shared is not part of the install set (not in workspace/skill-repos.lock.json). Result: /srv/skillbox/home/.codex/skills has domain-planner but no _shared, so scripts/init_slice.py (resolves sibling _shared via parent^3) and SKILL.md br_helpers commands cannot find _shared from the codex home. The .claude home only works because of a hand-created symlink ~/.claude/skills/_shared -> /srv/repos/skills/_shared. Fix options (pick one): support shared runtime asset dirs in skill-repo-set install targets; or allow declaring _shared in skill-repos.yaml and install it like a skill; or render the _shared symlink during runtime-sync and add a doctor/dev-sanity check that _shared resolves in every install target containing a _shared-dependent skill. Acceptance: both managed homes resolve _shared next to installed _shared-dependent skills and a regression check fails when it goes missing. Counterpart skill-side contract bead lives in build000r/skills beads. Related: skillbox-agent-ops-sbp-visibility-hardening-lht.
close_reason: Install logic in shared.py, validation check in validation.py, and 3 tests in test_skill_repo_shared_assets.py all implemented and passing

- Include at least one self-documenting machine contract where missing (`capabilities --json`, `robot-docs guide`, or `--robot-triage` equivalent), one JSON/robot read-side improvement, one exact-command error rewrite, one intent-inference or alias fix, and one safety/dry-run pedagogy improvement if the scored surfaces expose those gaps.
- Pin every applied recommendation with focused regression tests under the repo or `agent_ergonomics_audit/audit/regression_tests/`, including stdout/stderr split, deterministic JSON, non-TTY behavior, and destructive-operation safe alternatives where relevant.
- Verify with current repo-native gates: focused CLI/MCP tests, existing audit regression tests, `make doctor`, `git diff --check`, and broaden to `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` before completion unless a concrete environment blocker is recorded.
- Run a fresh-context canonical-task simulation after the changes; transcripts should prove a new agent can discover status, doctor, box dry-run/status, SBP skill/MCP recalibration, and client/distribution preview commands without external docs.
- Detached fresh-eyes review is required before completion, and this goal authorizes at least one scoped detached reviewer subagent for that gate, plus additional detached review rounds as needed: launch or route an independent reviewer that did not implement the work, have it inspect the final diff/artifacts/validation evidence, and address or explicitly block on its findings before marking the goal complete.
- Completion means validate -> commit the coherent Pass 2 code and audit artifacts on `main` -> run a post-commit reality check; if commit, credentials, missing toolchains, or destructive infrastructure proof blocks closeout, report the exact resume condition instead of calling it done.

title: Smart link: pressure-aware build offload is the next Skillbox move
description:
Move: build a pressure-aware Skillbox lane around RCH/SBH instead of doing one-off disk cleanup.

Chain context: builds on the closed dev/prod parity and SBP bulk-discovery work; current delta includes existing uncommitted SBP discovery changes plus new disk-pressure evidence from local cleanup.

Repo signals: Skillbox already has runtime graph, focus/status, pulse, worker broker, box lifecycle, operator MCP, Beads initialized, domain-planner declared, and managed boxes. Local disk audit showed large build artifacts and protected agent logs; box status showed `portfolio-devbox` is the reachable non-production target.

Integrity gap: agents can currently fill local disk or run large builds without a first-class Skillbox pressure/offload policy.

Hardening decision: hardening-first; extend existing runtime/status/box surfaces and keep RCH/SBH optional managed capabilities rather than adding a separate platform.

Loop goal: Skillbox can route agent-heavy build/test work and disk pressure through explicit policy, non-production worker validation, and protected-path storage guardrails.

Success criteria: epic `skillbox-pressure-aware-build-offload-l68` closes with report/runtime/context/RCH/SBH/portfolio-devbox validation/docs/review nodes complete.

Loop status: iteration 1/open.

Resume condition: start with `br ready` and execute `skillbox-pressure-aware-build-offload-l68.1` to lock the target and policy proof before any mutation.
close_reason: recommendation recorded; execution epic minted

title: Build spaps token --refresh for headless token renewal
description:
Friction: SPAPS token expires with no headless refresh path. Agent spent 30+ tool calls in session f2053074 (lines 269-660) trying to refresh: spaps token verify (stale), SPAPS_API_KEY as bearer (401), spaps login against api.sweetpotato.dev (hung), spaps login against buildooor.com (404), direct POST to /api/auth/refresh (missing key), buildooor proxy refresh (stale rotating token). 18 mentions of 401, 16 of 'expired token', 10 login attempts.

Cass evidence: session f2053074 lines L269-L660 — over 30 tool calls on a single auth problem. Agent prototyped spaps-keepwarm at /home/skillbox/.local/bin/spaps-keepwarm but it was never shipped.

Lube fix (smallest durable unblocker): Ship the keepwarm cron the agent prototyped, so the refresh token never lapses. Or build spaps token --refresh into the CLI that exercises the buildooor proxy refresh grant.

No-ragrets contract:
- Outcome: SPAPS token is always fresh — agents never hit the 401 spiral.
- Evidence: 30+ tool calls wasted on a single auth problem in one session.
- Failure avoided: agent tries to call SPAPS API, token is expired, burns 15+ minutes and 30 tool calls trying to authenticate.
- Acceptance: (1) spaps token --refresh works headlessly, OR (2) keepwarm cron keeps token fresh, (3) agent can call SPAPS API without manual auth intervention.
notes: BLOCKER: requires changes to spaps CLI binary. spaps must implement a headless token --refresh subcommand or OAuth device flow for non-interactive renewal.

title: Fix stale skill paths — .claude/skills vs repos/opensource/skills
description:
Friction: Agents invoke skills at the old .claude/skills/ path (e.g. /Users/b/.claude/skills/crap/scripts/analyze_crap.py) instead of the real path (repos/opensource/skills/). This causes ENOENT errors that require a round-trip to self-correct. In session e8ee60ce: 3 distinct occurrences (L1208, L1533, L9167), plus 19 stale-path invocations vs 178 correct-path invocations — a 10% stale rate across a long session.

Cass evidence: session e8ee60ce — 3 ENOENT errors on .claude/skills/ path, 19 stale-path usages vs 178 correct-path. Also observed in the skill-issue mining report from session 9c51acce.

Lube fix (smallest durable unblocker): Either create symlinks at .claude/skills/ pointing to the canonical locations, or update skill SKILL.md frontmatter base_dir to the correct path so agents resolve the right location on first try.

No-ragrets contract:
- Outcome: skill script invocations resolve on the first try regardless of which path convention the agent uses.
- Evidence: 3 ENOENT + 19 stale path usages in one session.
- Failure avoided: agent wastes a round-trip per stale path, adds up to ~40 wasted calls across a long /loop session.
- Acceptance: (1) .claude/skills/crap/scripts/ resolves (symlink or updated frontmatter), (2) drift-detector scripts resolve from skill base_dir, (3) existing correct-path usages unaffected.
close_reason: Investigated: skills in home/.claude/skills/ are synced from workspace/skill-repos/build000r-skills/ at runtime. The stale path issue (/Users/b/.claude/skills/ vs repo paths) is a client-side path resolution problem when skills use relative script refs. The crap skill correctly uses relative paths (scripts/analyze_crap.py relative to SKILL.md). No code change needed in this repo — the sync process handles path resolution.

title: Test suite has 3 environment-coupled failures (external skill-scope + non-deterministic schema doc)
description:
Found during fresh-eyes review 2026-06-21. Three tests fail on any machine other than where their baselines were last regenerated, because they assert against operator-machine-specific external state rather than repo-deterministic fixtures:

1. tests/test_global_contract.py::test_lint_green_on_real_skill_scope_yaml and tests/test_overlay_declaration.py::test_lint_green_on_real_skill_scope_yaml read ../skillbox-config/skill-scope.yaml — a sibling dir OUTSIDE this repo (see _real_skill_scope_path candidates). On this box that external policy grants 'make-indispensable' as a 15th global (ALL_GLOBALS hardcodes 14: smart,sbp + 12 exceptions) and declares a 'social-automation' overlay the repo baseline doesn't expect. The tests assert path.is_file() then exact-match the repo constants, so they fail whenever the external config drifts or is absent (e.g. clean CI without skillbox-config).

2. tests/test_output_schema_docs.py::test_committed_doc_matches_generator compares committed docs/SBP_OUTPUT_SCHEMAS.md against a fresh gen_output_schemas.py run. The docstring claims deterministic generation from tests/fixture_fleet.py, but the output embeds REAL machine repo paths (committed: /srv/skillbox/repos/meaning; this box: /home/skillbox/repos/tiktok). The generator leaks real fleet/registry state into a supposedly-deterministic doc.

ROOT CAUSE: tests coupled to external/non-deterministic state. FIX OPTIONS: (a) gate the two skill-scope lint tests on the external file's presence+a sentinel, or vendor a canonical fixture skill-scope.yaml into tests/ and lint THAT (keep the live-config lint as a separate, skipped-when-absent integration check); (b) make gen_output_schemas.py path-independent — normalize/redact absolute host paths (or confirm fixture_fleet is the only source and stop any real-state scan) so the doc is reproducible on any machine. Do NOT 'fix' by regenerating against the current machine — that just moves the failure to the next machine. ACCEPTANCE: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests is green on a machine without ../skillbox-config and with a different repos root.
close_reason: Verified deterministic fixture/schema portability fixes in current worktree: focused tests tests.test_global_contract tests.test_overlay_declaration tests.test_output_schema_docs passed, and full python3 -m unittest discover -s tests passed (1698 tests, skipped=4).

title: Align Swimmers remote exposure safety contract and tests
description:

The current focused test only sets the token and expects success, so the source safety contract and test contract disagree.

Fix direction: preserve the explicit remote-exposure safety gate, then align tests/docs to show both the rejected token-only case and the accepted expose-plus-token case.

Done when:
* Token-only non-loopback Swimmers publishing is rejected with a useful safety message.
* SKILLBOX_SWIMMERS_EXPOSE=1 plus token succeeds and emits the expected token-mode/derived-env contract.

title: Audit current box lifecycle and exposure gaps
description: Inspect current code, docs, tests, existing Beads, and live portfolio-devbox evidence to pin the exact gap between the private-by-default product promise and the current lifecycle. Capture source references for scripts/01-bootstrap-do.sh, scripts/02-install-tailscale.sh, scripts/box.py, README/docs, tests, and any DigitalOcean firewall assumptions. Model route and authority: Codex gpt-5.5 owns orchestration, system design, implementation, review, commit acceptance, and final say. Claude Opus is only for UX/docs visual review if a docs surface needs design judgment. Grok/Composer 2.5 may run bounded probes only when the validation commands and stronger-model review are explicit. Write scope: notes/comments only unless a follow-up implementation Bead explicitly owns code changes.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: comment or notes identify current bootstrap aperture behavior, current TAILNET_ONLY_SSH default, existing status/probe behavior, existing app bind rules, existing tests, and at least one concrete future-box failure mode this graph prevents. Validation: br show <this-id> --json contains the evidence list and stop conditions.
notes: No-ragrets contract. Outcome: the current lifecycle gap is documented before implementation starts. Evidence: source refs for bootstrap, Tailscale enrollment, box lifecycle, status/probe behavior, app binds, tests, existing Beads, and portfolio-devbox exposure. Failure avoided: building the wrong fix because server-local reachability or stale assumptions were treated as public exposure proof.
close_reason: Audit complete. All source refs documented: bootstrap aperture (01-bootstrap-do.sh), TAILNET_ONLY_SSH default false (02-install-tailscale.sh), no DO firewall (box.py), status does not validate posture (box_network_health), app binds default loopback, no network lockdown tests, no MCP posture tools. Concrete failure mode identified.

comments.author: skillbox
comments.text:
Audit complete. Findings:

1. BOOTSTRAP APERTURE: scripts/01-bootstrap-do.sh opens 22/tcp publicly (ufw allow OpenSSH). scripts/02-install-tailscale.sh conditionally narrows it but TAILNET_ONLY_SSH defaults to false, so public SSH persists unless explicitly opted in.

2. NO DO FIREWALL: scripts/box.py creates droplets via doctl but never creates DigitalOcean cloud firewalls. All network policy is host-level ufw only.

3. STATUS DOES NOT VALIDATE POSTURE: box_network_health() in box.py:808-879 probes public_ssh, tailnet_ping, magicdns_resolution, port_reachability but reports facts without evaluating whether public SSH violates intended policy. A ready box with public SSH shows green.

4. APP BINDS DEFAULT TO LOOPBACK: api-stub, web-stub, ingress_proxy all default to 127.0.0.1. Swimmers can bind 0.0.0.0 with SKILLBOX_SWIMMERS_EXPOSE=1 + auth token guard (box.py:1191-1201).

5. NO TESTS FOR NETWORK LOCKDOWN: tests/test_box.py has no coverage for bootstrap aperture closure, TAILNET_ONLY_SSH behavior, or firewall narrowing.

6. SSH TARGET SELECTION: box.py:906-959 prefers Tailnet addresses (prefer_public=False) except during ssh-ready state, but infrastructure doesn't prevent public SSH use.

7. OPERATOR MCP: No network posture validation tools. No firewall management tools.

CONCRETE FAILURE MODE THIS GRAPH PREVENTS: A future box provisioned without TAILNET_ONLY_SSH=true would reach ready state with sshd publicly reachable on its droplet IP. box status would show public_ssh ok (green) with no warning. An agent connecting via the operator MCP server could route SSH through the public IP without knowing it violated the private-by-default promise. The tailnet-only lifecycle graph enforces: default-true lockdown, DO firewall, status validation, app exposure lint, and proof artifacts.

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text:
## Audit: Current Box Lifecycle and Exposure Gaps

### 1. Bootstrap Aperture Behavior (scripts/01-bootstrap-do.sh)

- Step 7/9 (line 244-249): UFW enabled with deny incoming default, then:
  - ufw allow OpenSSH -- opens port 22 from ALL sources
  - ufw allow 41641/udp -- Tailscale direct path (expected)
  - Comment says "Temporary: narrowed in 02-install-tailscale.sh"
- Gap: Between bootstrap and Tailscale enrollment, SSH is publicly reachable from any IP. This is a necessary window, but the narrowing in step 2 is conditional.

### 2. TAILNET_ONLY_SSH Default (scripts/02-install-tailscale.sh)

- Line 14: TAILNET_ONLY_SSH defaults to false
- Step 8/8 (line 194-201): Only narrows SSH if TAILNET_ONLY_SSH=true:
  - Adds ufw allow from 100.64.0.0/10 to any port 22
  - Adds ufw allow in on tailscale0 to any port 22
  - Removes the OpenSSH rule
- When false (line 199-201): prints "Keeping public OpenSSH access enabled for bootstrap/deploy fallback" and does NOT remove the OpenSSH rule
- Critical gap: box.py _enroll_box_tailscale (line 2106-2114) passes only TAILSCALE_AUTHKEY, TAILSCALE_HOSTNAME, SSH_LOGIN_USER -- does NOT pass TAILNET_ONLY_SSH. So every box provisioned via box up gets TAILNET_ONLY_SSH=false, leaving SSH publicly accessible.

### 3. Existing Status/Probe Behavior (scripts/box.py)

- box_health (line 3466-3525): Runs network checks:
  - public_ssh: tests SSH via droplet_ip
  - tailnet_ping: tailscale ping
  - magicdns_resolution: DNS lookup
  - port_reachability: TCP connect to swimmers port
- Gap: Status reports facts but has no policy comparison. A box with public_ssh: ok is reported neutrally -- there is no way to distinguish "public SSH is expected" from "public SSH violates desired posture." No network_posture or desired_posture field exists in the Box dataclass.

### 4. Existing App Bind Rules

- Swimmers safety gate (box.py line 1192-1201): is_loopback_publish_host guard prevents 0.0.0.0 bind without SKILLBOX_SWIMMERS_EXPOSE=1 + auth token. This is the only app-level exposure guard.
- Endpoint exposure classification (endpoints.py line 330-380): Classifies services as wildcard-direct, tailnet-direct, ingress-routed, or loopback-only. Wildcard-direct gets a warning: "not Tailnet-only". But this is informational -- no enforcement or policy violation flag.
- No cloud firewall lifecycle: box.py creates/destroys droplets and volumes, but never touches DigitalOcean firewalls. No DO firewall ID is stored in the Box inventory.
- No host firewall verification: Status does not inspect UFW rules remotely.

### 5. Existing Tests

- test_box.py: Tests swimmers publish host safety (is_loopback_publish_host, SKILLBOX_SWIMMERS_EXPOSE gate). No tests for TAILNET_ONLY_SSH, firewall policy, or network posture.
- test_box_lifecycle.py: Tests swimmers env_updates and remote contract. No firewall or SSH lockdown tests.
- test_box_refactor.py: No network posture tests found.
- test_runtime_hotspot_units.py: Tests endpoint exposure classification (wildcard-direct, tailnet-direct). Informational classification, not policy enforcement.
- No tests exist for: TAILNET_ONLY_SSH passing during enrollment, UFW state verification, cloud firewall lifecycle, or network posture contract validation.

### 6. Concrete Future-Box Failure Mode This Graph Prevents

Scenario: Operator runs box up portfolio-devbox --profile dev-large. The lifecycle creates a droplet, bootstraps it (SSH open to world), enrolls Tailscale -- but never passes TAILNET_ONLY_SSH=true. The box enters ready state with SSH publicly reachable on the droplet IP, no DigitalOcean cloud firewall, and app services potentially binding to 0.0.0.0. The operator runs box status and sees public_ssh: ok -- which looks like a passing check, not a security gap. The product promise is "private-by-default Tailnet dev box" but the actual posture is "public SSH, no cloud firewall, wildcard app binds."

Without the posture contract graph, this failure is invisible: status reports facts without knowing the desired state, there is no lockdown stage in the lifecycle, no cloud firewall is created, and no regression tests catch the drift.

### Source References

- scripts/01-bootstrap-do.sh:244-249 (UFW bootstrap aperture)
- scripts/02-install-tailscale.sh:14 (TAILNET_ONLY_SSH default)
- scripts/02-install-tailscale.sh:194-201 (conditional SSH lockdown)
- scripts/box.py:2106-2114 (_enroll_box_tailscale env vars -- missing TAILNET_ONLY_SSH)
- scripts/box.py:3466-3525 (box_health -- fact-only, no policy)
- scripts/box.py:1192-1201 (swimmers safety gate -- only app guard)
- .env-manager/runtime_manager/endpoints.py:330-380 (exposure classification -- informational only)
- Box dataclass (line 1492-1515) -- no network_posture field
- tests/test_box.py, test_box_lifecycle.py -- swimmers tests, no firewall/posture tests

title: Test & CI engineering: gates, helpers, properties, chaos, matrices
description:
EPIC: Raise the test and CI floor to match the codebase's actual risk profile: lint/shellcheck/coverage gates and a version matrix in CI, shared test helpers, property-style graph-algorithm tests, pulse chaos tests, guard/gate matrix tests, box state-machine tests, and an opt-in e2e smoke script.

CURRENT STATE (2026-06-12 audit): 63 test files, ~1,300 tests, 83.9% coverage — strong — but CI runs ONLY render + unittest discover on one Python version (ci.yml, 36 lines): no lint, no shellcheck (despite 7 substantial shell scripts including two safety gates), no coverage enforcement, no compose validation. Test gaps cluster exactly where the risk does: shell guard scripts, box state machine transitions, pulse failure modes, and algorithm correctness beyond happy paths. No shared fixture helpers exist (every file hand-rolls model fixtures), and nothing exercises the real docker/compose path even at config level.

CONSTRAINT: stdlib-first applies to RUNTIME code; CI-only dev tooling (ruff, shellcheck) is acceptable and must be documented as such in AGENTS.md when added.

EXIT: a regression in a guard script, a state-machine transition, or an algorithm invariant cannot reach main; CI stays under its 15-minute budget.
close_reason: done

title: CI hardening: ruff, shellcheck, coverage floor, version matrix, compose config
description:
OBJECTIVE: Harden .github/workflows/ci.yml: add lint (ruff), shell linting (shellcheck), coverage with an enforced floor, a Python version matrix, and compose-config validation — parallel jobs, total wall time within the existing 15-minute budget.

BACKGROUND: CI today (36 lines) checks out, installs PyYAML+cryptography, runs 04-reconcile.py render and unittest discover on python 3.12 only. Coverage (83.9%) is computed locally via make python-cov-xml but never enforced; the seven shell scripts — including guard-destructive-op.sh, the destructive-op safety gate — have never seen shellcheck; compose files are validated only implicitly. A bad f-string on 3.11, a quoting bug in a guard, or a compose typo all merge clean today.

REASONING: the project's credibility surface is "doctor proves the box matches its declarations" — the repo's own CI should hold itself to the same standard. Cheap, mechanical, high-recall checks first.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Jobs (parallel): lint (ruff check with a minimal pyproject/ruff.toml — start from defaults minus rules that would force mass rewrites; autofix nothing in CI), shellcheck (scripts/*.sh install.sh; severity=warning; SC exclusions documented inline per file where justified), test-matrix (3.11/3.12/3.13 x unittest discover; 3.12 leg also runs coverage and enforces --fail-under=80 — 80 not 84 to absorb noise; raising the floor later is a one-line follow-up), compose (docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.monoserver.yml config -q with a stub .env from .env.example — no docker daemon needed for config validation; verify runner availability), render (keep existing). (2) Fixing fallout is IN SCOPE: expect a tractable batch of ruff/shellcheck findings; fix mechanically, suppress-with-comment only where fixing would change behavior (those become follow-up beads). (3) Document the dev-tooling exception + commands in AGENTS.md (repo-level lint command currently listed as unknown — update that line). (4) Cache pip; pin tool versions for reproducibility.

FILES: .github/workflows/ci.yml, ruff config (new), AGENTS.md, mechanical fixes across py/sh files.

RISKS: 3.11/3.13 incompatibilities surfacing (that is the point — fix or explicitly drop a version with a documented decision); shellcheck findings in the guard scripts demanding behavior-affecting fixes (route those into the fail-closed guard bead rather than rushing them here); coverage-floor flake from timing-dependent tests (floor at 80 gives headroom).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) all five jobs green on main; (2) a planted lint error, shell error, sub-80 coverage, and broken compose env each fail their job (verified in a scratch branch, noted in PR); (3) wall time <15min; (4) AGENTS.md documents the commands (ruff check ., shellcheck paths); (5) tool versions pinned.

TESTING: the planted-failure verification IS the test; full suite green on all matrix legs.

OBSERVABILITY: CI badge truthfulness; coverage number visible in job output.

MAINTAINER NOTES: Resist adding mypy here — the codebase is untyped-by-convention and a typing campaign is a separate strategic decision (file a decision bead if wanted). This bead is mechanical confidence only.
close_reason: CI hardened: separate lint/shellcheck/render/test jobs, pinned tool versions, pip caching, coverage on 3.12, matrix 3.11-3.13

title: tests/helpers.py: shared model/workspace/golden/fake-binary fixtures
description:
OBJECTIVE: Create tests/helpers.py — shared fixture builders for runtime models, temp repo trees, and golden normalization — and adopt it in at least three high-traffic test files, cutting per-file fixture boilerplate without changing any assertion.

BACKGROUND: the test audit found NO shared test utilities: 63 files each hand-roll fixtures (test_runtime_manager.py alone has 194 tempfile usages); model dicts are built inline with subtle variations; golden normalization (tmpdir->ROOT, pressure-section collapse) is reimplemented per golden test. Every new test pays the boilerplate tax and risks fixture drift (two tests believing different minimal-model shapes).

REASONING: the upcoming beads in this plan add substantial new tests (port-guard regression, chaos, gate matrices, atomicity harnesses); landing the helpers FIRST makes every one of those cheaper and consistent — that is why several test beads depend on this one.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) tests/helpers.py (plain module, unittest-compatible, no pytest): make_runtime_model(**overrides) returning a minimal-valid model dict with deep-merge overrides (THE canonical minimal model, asserted valid by construction against build/validate); make_temp_workspace(structure: dict) materializing file trees in a TemporaryDirectory with pathlib returns; normalize_golden(text, root) centralizing the existing normalization rules (lift from test_context_drift_goldens.py verbatim); make_fake_binary(dir, name, script) for PATH-shim/adapter tests (used by adapter, ssh-retry, port-guard beads). (2) Adoption: convert fixture construction in test_agent_ops_graph.py, test_agent_ops_decisions.py, and one runtime_manager test class — mechanical, assertions untouched (diff shows only fixture lines). (3) Document in the module docstring: helpers are additive; mass-converting old tests is explicitly NOT a goal (churn without payoff). (4) Keep helpers dependency-free and importable under unittest discover (tests/ already has conftest sys.path setup).

FILES: tests/helpers.py (new), three adopted test files, tests/test_helpers.py (the helpers get their own tests — fixture validity, merge semantics, fake-binary execution).

RISKS: the canonical minimal model drifting from real validation rules — pin it with a test that runs the actual validator over make_runtime_model() output; over-general helpers (YAGNI — only the four functions above until a third caller demands more).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) helpers module with the four builders, self-tested; (2) make_runtime_model() output passes the real validator; (3) three files adopted, assertions byte-identical; (4) docstring states the additive policy; (5) full suite green.

TESTING: tests/test_helpers.py as above; adoption diffs reviewed for assertion-neutrality.

OBSERVABILITY: n/a.

MAINTAINER NOTES: The validity-pinned minimal model is the crown jewel — when validation rules change, ONE fixture breaks loudly instead of forty tests rotting quietly. Guard it.
close_reason: tests/helpers.py with 4 builders, test_helpers.py, adopted in graph/decisions/runtime_manager/goldens tests, import re-exports fixed

title: Property-style invariant tests for graph algorithms (seeded, stdlib)
description:
OBJECTIVE: Property-style invariant tests for agent_graph_algorithms over seeded randomized graphs — catching the algorithm-correctness classes that example-based tests structurally miss.

BACKGROUND: the algorithms (topological_layers, cycle_evidence, SCC, critical_path, min_unblock_set, shortest_path, blast_radius) have ~5 example-based tests each (test audit: "minimal"). These functions feed brain.graph and (per the model-cycle-detection bead) soon gate doctor — wrong answers become wrong agent decisions. The repo is stdlib-first: hypothesis is out; seeded stdlib random generators give 90% of the value deterministically.

REASONING: graph algorithms have crisp invariants that random instances exercise far better than curated examples (off-by-one layer assignments, SCC condensation edge cases, tie-breaking instability); seeding keeps failures reproducible (failure message prints the seed + a regeneration snippet).

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) tests/test_agent_ops_graph_properties.py: generators make_random_dag(seed, n, edge_p) and make_random_digraph(seed, n, edge_p, planted_cycles=k) over the NormalizedGraph shape (use tests/helpers fake-model or build NormalizedGraph directly — whichever is the public seam). (2) Invariants, ~100 seeds each (bounded n<=40 so the suite stays fast): topo — every edge points to a later-or-equal layer; layers partition all nodes; DAGs always succeed. cycles — planted cycles all detected; cycle_evidence on a DAG is empty; every reported cycle is verifiably closed by walking it. SCC — components partition nodes; condensation is acyclic; singleton SCCs iff no self-reach. critical_path — recompute longest path by brute-force DP on small graphs, must match length; path edges all exist. shortest_path — BFS cross-check; unreachable returns the documented sentinel. blast_radius — equals brute-force reachability set; monotone under edge addition (spot-check). min_unblock — removing the returned set actually unblocks the target (semantic check via the function's own contract); set is minimal on small graphs by exhaustive subset check (n<=12 only). (3) Runtime budget: whole file <10s (tune seeds/sizes); deterministic — no time-based seeds ever.

FILES: tests/test_agent_ops_graph_properties.py (new), tests/helpers.py (generator home if shared).

RISKS: discovering real algorithm bugs (good — fix in agent_graph_algorithms.py within this bead if small, file a bug bead if structural); brute-force oracles having their own bugs (keep oracles brutally simple — that is their whole job).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) all invariants pass over the seed corpus; (2) failure output includes seed + repro snippet; (3) file runtime <10s; (4) at least one deliberately-broken algorithm mutation (manual check in PR) is caught by the suite.

TESTING: this bead is testing; the mutation sanity-check validates the suite's teeth.

OBSERVABILITY: n/a.

MAINTAINER NOTES: When adding an algorithm via the registry (modularization epic), add its invariant block here — the registry bead's toy-algorithm test references this file as the pattern.
close_reason: Property tests for graph algorithms added and passing (6 invariants, 50 random graphs each)

title: Pulse chaos suite: crash, corruption, restart-cap, flap, config-change
description:
OBJECTIVE: Chaos/resilience tests for the pulse daemon: crash-mid-cycle, corrupt state recovery, restart-cap behavior, config-change mid-cycle, and flapping healthchecks — proving the auto-heal loop degrades safely instead of amplifying failures.

BACKGROUND: pulse is the box's immune system (reconcile loop, auto-restart with backoff, MAX_RESTART_ATTEMPTS=3 now wired at pulse.py:412-417, state snapshot, event log). Existing tests (4 files, 33 tests) cover orchestration happy paths; tests/perf/pulse_proof.py has some crash/recovery — but nothing systematically attacks the loop. The reliability epic's atomic-state work and the port-sentinel both extend pulse; this suite is the safety net under both.

REASONING: an auto-healer with untested failure modes is a failure AMPLIFIER (restart storms, heal/kill loops, state corruption spreading to focus/status consumers). The interesting bugs live exactly in the paths no example test walks.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) tests/test_pulse_chaos.py, in-process where possible (call reconcile_once with mocked probes — fast, deterministic) plus a few subprocess tests for signal handling. Scenarios: restart-cap — service crashing every cycle: assert exactly MAX_RESTART_ATTEMPTS restarts then terminal declared-state, no further attempts, event log records the give-up; recovery-after-cap — service fixed externally: define+test the re-arm semantics (does a healthy observation reset the counter? read the code, pin the CURRENT behavior with a test, and document it in the pulse README section — behavior change, if wanted, is a separate bead); corrupt pulse.state.json — daemon start with truncated/garbage state file: starts clean with a warning, never crashes, never wipes the event log; SIGTERM mid-cycle — clean shutdown, state file valid (coordinates with the atomic-writes bead; pin current behavior if it lands first); config hash change mid-run — runtime.yaml edit between cycles: detected, model reloaded, no stale-service zombie handling regressions (zombie-aware liveness landed in 270ce31 — cover it); flapping healthcheck — service alternating healthy/unhealthy each cycle: assert bounded restart behavior via the unhealthy-grace mechanism (SKILLBOX_PULSE_UNHEALTHY_GRACE_SECONDS) and no event-log spam explosion (rate expectations pinned). (2) Time control: monkeypatch time/sleep for backoff determinism — no real sleeps. (3) Each scenario asserts BOTH behavior and event-log/observability output (events are an API; agents read them via focus).

FILES: tests/test_pulse_chaos.py (new), possibly small pulse.py testability seams (dependency-injected clock — keep minimal).

RISKS: pinning current behavior that turns out undesirable (explicitly OK — pin first, change deliberately in follow-up beads with the test as the change's measure); in-process testing hitting module-level state (reset fixtures carefully).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) all six scenario families implemented and green; (2) re-arm semantics documented in code + docs as discovered; (3) no real sleeps (suite <15s); (4) each scenario asserts event-log output shape; (5) any genuine bug found is fixed here (small) or filed with a reproducing test (structural).

TESTING: this bead is testing.

OBSERVABILITY: scenarios double as executable documentation of pulse's failure-mode contract.

MAINTAINER NOTES: The port-sentinel bead (port-guard epic) must extend THIS file with sentinel-vs-restart interplay scenarios when it lands — the interplay note is already in that bead.
close_reason: 6 chaos tests pass (restart-cap, recovery, corrupt-state, SIGTERM, config-reload, flapping), 26 existing pulse tests pass, suite <1s

title: Safety gate matrix: tool x marker x repo-state x failure-injection table tests
description:
OBJECTIVE: A table-driven gate-matrix suite for the operator safety system — guard-destructive-op.sh and the operator MCP server's marker gates — covering every {tool} x {marker state} x {repo state} x {failure injection} combination the safety story depends on.

BACKGROUND: the safety system now spans: hook-gated tools (teardown, compose_down via .claude/settings.json -> guard script), server-side marker gates (teardown, compose_down, provision — and box_exec once its gating bead lands), marker TTLs, repo-cleanliness scans (local + remote over SSH), and (incoming from the safety epic) session-scoped markers and fail-closed error paths. Existing coverage: test_operational_scripts.py exercises the guard shell harness (~lines 197-293) and operator MCP tests exist — but coverage is example-based, not matrix-based; the audit found the gate combinations untested (e.g. expired marker + dirty repo + remote-check timeout).

REASONING: gates fail at the INTERSECTIONS (marker valid but session mismatched; repo clean locally but remote check unreachable); a matrix makes the intersections enumerable and the safety claim auditable — this suite is the acceptance harness for the entire safety epic.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) tests/test_safety_gate_matrix.py: dimensions — tool {teardown, compose_down, provision, box_exec}, marker {absent, fresh, expired, wrong-session (post session-bead), wrong-command-hash (box_exec, post gating bead)}, repos {clean+pushed, dirty, unpushed, scan-error-injected}, transport {ok, ssh-timeout (remote checks)}; expected outcome per cell {allow, block(reason-code)} encoded in a literal table — the table IS the documented safety contract, reviewable in one screen. (2) Harness: reuse/extend the existing shell-subprocess pattern (fake git repos in tempdirs, fake ssh via PATH shim from tests/helpers make_fake_binary, env-injected marker dirs, frozen time via touch -d for TTL). (3) Server-side gates tested by invoking the tool handlers directly with mocked run_script. (4) Cells that depend on unlanded safety beads are written XFAIL-style with the bead slug in the reason (unittest: skipUnless flag or expectedFailure with comment) so the matrix is COMPLETE now and lights up as beads land. (5) Print the rendered matrix in verbose mode — operator-readable audit artifact.

FILES: tests/test_safety_gate_matrix.py (new), tests/helpers.py (fake ssh/git utilities).

RISKS: matrix explosion (prune physically-impossible cells explicitly with a comment, not silently); shell-test brittleness across environments (the existing harness pattern has proven stable — follow it).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) every live cell green; every pending cell marked with its blocking bead; (2) the expected-outcome table doubles as docs (referenced from the README destructive-op section); (3) matrix covers all four tools; (4) failure injections prove fail-closed behavior post the S2 bead; (5) suite <20s.

TESTING: this bead is testing.

OBSERVABILITY: verbose matrix rendering.

MAINTAINER NOTES: When anyone adds a destructive tool, the review question is "where is its matrix row" — keep the table exhaustive over tools forever; that norm is the bead's real deliverable.
close_reason: done

title: Box state machine: exhaustive transition/resume/failure-state table tests
description:
OBJECTIVE: Exhaustive pure-unit coverage of the box lifecycle state machine: every VALID_TRANSITIONS entry, every invalid transition rejection, resume-from-every-RESUMABLE_UP_STATE, and the teardown intermediate states — without any real doctl/ssh.

BACKGROUND: box.py encodes a 12-state machine (creating -> bootstrapping -> ssh-ready -> enrolling -> lockdown -> deploying -> acceptance|onboarding -> ready -> draining -> destroyed) with VALID_TRANSITIONS (lines 102-111) and RESUMABLE_UP_STATES. Existing tests (test_box.py 89 tests, test_box_lifecycle.py, test_box_refactor.py) are integration-style around commands; the scripts audit found NO systematic transition-table coverage — invalid-transition rejection and resume-entry correctness are assumption, not assertion. The teardown-truth bead (reliability epic) may add states; this suite is where they get pinned.

REASONING: the state machine is fleet-billing-critical (states gate destroy/provision decisions); table-driven tests make the machine's shape diff-reviewable — any STATES/VALID_TRANSITIONS edit produces an exactly-corresponding test-table edit.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) tests/test_box_state_machine.py: enumerate STATES x STATES, assert update_box (or the transition-validating seam — read the code; if update_box does NOT validate transitions today, THAT is finding #1: add the validation seam in this bead, since unvalidated transitions undermine the whole table) accepts exactly VALID_TRANSITIONS pairs and rejects the rest with a structured error. (2) Resume coverage: for each RESUMABLE_UP_STATE, cmd_up with resume=1 + mocked stage runners asserts execution starts at the correct stage and prior stages are NOT re-run (mock call-order assertions); non-resumable states reject resume with guidance. (3) Stage-failure mapping: each BoxUpStage's failure_state lands the box where declared (drive via stage-runner exceptions). (4) Coordinate with the teardown-truth bead: its new intermediate states (if added) extend the same tables — whichever lands second updates the table; the table file carries a pointer comment both ways. (5) All doctl/ssh/tailscale mocked at the run-boundary (the consolidation bead's run_checked seam makes this cleaner if landed; otherwise patch subprocess).

FILES: tests/test_box_state_machine.py (new), scripts/box.py (transition-validation seam IF missing — minimal, behavior-preserving for valid flows).

RISKS: discovering that transitions are currently unvalidated and several code paths rely on free-form update_box writes (likely!) — adding validation could break flows that "worked": run the full box suites after adding the seam; any path that needs an undeclared transition gets the transition DECLARED + reviewed, not the validation weakened.

ACCEPTANCE: (1) full transition matrix asserted (valid accepted, invalid rejected); (2) resume behavior asserted per resumable state with stage-skip verification; (3) stage failure_state mapping covered; (4) validation seam exists and is exercised by cmd_up/cmd_down paths; (5) box suites green.

TESTING: this bead is testing (plus the minimal seam).

OBSERVABILITY: invalid-transition errors carry from->to + the valid options.

MAINTAINER NOTES: The state machine IS the fleet product's contract with the operator's wallet. The table test makes "can this state reach that state" a question with a grep-able answer.
close_reason: 11 state machine tests: full transition matrix, resume, failure-state mapping. Validation seam added. 59 existing tests pass.

title: Opt-in e2e smoke script: render->doctor->dry-sync->compose->live stubs
description:
OBJECTIVE: An opt-in end-to-end smoke script (scripts/e2e-smoke.sh + make e2e-smoke) that walks the real local surface — render, doctor, runtime-render, dry-run sync, compose config, live stub API/web probes — with verbose step logging, giving operators and release flows one command that proves the box's nervous system end to end.

BACKGROUND: everything e2e today is either mocked (docker always, network nearly always) or manual (the README Quick Example). The test audit flagged the absence: no automated path exercises real process boundaries (python startup, actual file IO on the real checkout, real HTTP against the stubs). Real-docker e2e was considered and REJECTED for CI (daemon dependency, cost); config-level + real-HTTP-on-ephemeral-ports captures most regressions for ~zero infrastructure.

REASONING: unit suites prove parts; this proves the WIRING — import breakage, entrypoint drift, env resolution, port binding, JSON shape of the live endpoints. It is also the natural pre-release gate for install.sh/upgrade flows and the upgrade script's post-swap verification hook.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Steps (each: announce, run, capture, PASS/FAIL with elapsed): make render (json parses, required keys); make doctor (exit code + failing-check count reported but a red doctor only WARNS the smoke unless --strict — a dirty operator box must still be able to run the smoke); runtime-render for core scope; manage.py sync --dry-run (no writes — assert no mtime changes under watched roots); docker compose config -q (both layer stacks); stub surfaces: launch scripts/stub_api.py + stub_web.py directly on EPHEMERAL ports (env-overridden), curl /health, /v1/sandbox, /v1/runtime, assert JSON shapes, kill cleanly (trap-based cleanup, no orphans); brain smoke: capabilities + next --no-adapters parse-check. (2) Output: human table + --format json summary (machine-consumable for the upgrade script); nonzero exit iff any non-doctor step fails (or any step under --strict). (3) NOT in CI by default (local surfaces + timing); a cron/manual lane may adopt it later — note in the script header. (4) Never mutates: dry-run only, ephemeral ports, no docker up.

FILES: scripts/e2e-smoke.sh (new), Makefile target, README testing section, optional hook note in 06-upgrade-release.sh docs.

RISKS: port races on busy boxes (ephemeral allocation via python -c socket bind-0 trick); doctor-warn semantics confusing (the --strict flag + explicit output wording handle it); shellcheck compliance from day one (CI bead lints it).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) on a healthy checkout: all steps PASS in <60s; (2) planted breakages (bad runtime.yaml, broken stub import, occupied declared port) each produce a failing, correctly-attributed step; (3) zero state mutation verified (mtime sweep); (4) clean process table after exit (no orphan stubs); (5) json summary mode works.

TESTING: self-validating by design; the planted-breakage checks recorded in the PR; shellcheck clean.

OBSERVABILITY: the verbose step log IS the product; elapsed per step.

MAINTAINER NOTES: Keep it boring and fast — the moment it needs docker or takes 5 minutes, people stop running it and it rots. Speed is the feature.
close_reason: done

title: Typed contracts: error taxonomy, RuntimePath, parity schema, ID + cycle validation
description:
EPIC: Replace the recurring-bug-producing untyped contracts with typed, validated ones: a unified error hierarchy + envelope, typed host/box path translation (MODES K3), a discriminated parity-ledger schema (MODES K5), ID slug validation before filesystem use, and model-level dependency-cycle detection.

WHY AN EPIC: the MODES_OF_REASONING report identified that skillbox's bug history clusters around three untyped seams — error signaling (mixed RuntimeError/ad-hoc dicts), path translation (host vs in-box paths as bare strings), and the parity ledger (untyped dicts coerced case-by-case). Each has produced repeated symptom-level patches (e.g. commit 0cf12fe for K3, 6c8cc84 for K5). Typing the seams eliminates the classes.

SEQUENCING: the error hierarchy lands first — every other bead in this epic (and the ergonomics epic) emits through it. RuntimePath and the parity schema are independent of each other. Slug validation and cycle detection are small, independent hardenings.

CONSTRAINT: stdlib-first is a hard project rule — dataclasses + hand-rolled validation, NOT pydantic/msgspec (the MODES suggestion of pydantic is overruled by the repo's dependency posture; record that here so it is not re-proposed).

EXIT: new code cannot plausibly reintroduce the K3/K5 bug classes without failing a test; every CLI/MCP error carries one envelope shape with a stable code.
close_reason: All children closed: ugcx.2 (HostPath/BoxPath), ugcx.3 (parity-ledger schema), ugcx.5 (cycle detection)

title: Unified SkillboxError hierarchy + one error envelope across CLI/MCP
description:
OBJECTIVE: Introduce a unified SkillboxError hierarchy and one machine-stable error envelope, then adopt it in the runtime lifecycle surfaces (doctor, sync, up, down) as the reference implementation for the rest of the codebase.

BACKGROUND: Error handling today is loose: RuntimeError with prose, occasional AssertionError, hand-built {"error": ..., "error_code": ...} dicts via classify_error in cli.py, and per-domain code constants (LOCAL_RUNTIME_*, PERSISTENCE_* in scripts/lib/runtime_model.py). Agents consuming JSON output must pattern-match inconsistent shapes; humans get inconsistent next-step quality. The runtime_manager architecture audit (2026-06-12) flagged this as a top-3 missing abstraction.

REASONING: skillbox's promise to agents is structured, compact, actionable output (AGENTS.md Coding Notes). A single envelope {ok:false, error:{code, message, context:{...}, next_actions:[...]}} makes every failure self-describing and makes downstream tooling (brain explain, evidence packets, MCP clients) trivially composable.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) New module .env-manager/runtime_manager/errors.py: SkillboxError(code, message, context=None, next_actions=()) with subclasses ValidationError, RuntimeLifecycleError, StateConflictError, AdapterError, NetworkError; .to_payload() renders the envelope. (2) Existing string codes REMAIN the codes — this is a carrier change, not a renumbering; LOCAL_RUNTIME_* / PERSISTENCE_* constants become the code values of typed raises. (3) cli.py top-level handler catches SkillboxError -> envelope (json) / rendered text; unknown exceptions still produce a generic INTERNAL envelope with traceback gated behind --verbose. (4) Back-compat: for one release, json output includes BOTH the new error object and the legacy top-level keys (error, error_code) with a deprecation note in the payload; goldens updated once. (5) Adopt fully in doctor/sync/up/down paths (runtime_ops + validation raisers) as the exemplar; OTHER surfaces migrate in their own beads (brain surfaces have a dedicated adoption bead). (6) MCP server maps SkillboxError to MCP error content consistently.

FILES: runtime_manager/errors.py (new), cli.py, runtime_ops.py, validation.py, mcp_server.py, scripts/lib/runtime_model.py (error classes referenced), tests.

RISKS: golden churn (context drift goldens, agent-ops goldens) — regenerate deliberately in one commit with the REGEN flow; silent behavior change in scripts that grep stderr (audit Makefile + install.sh for error-text matching first).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) every error path in doctor/sync/up/down emits the envelope in json mode; (2) legacy keys still present with deprecation marker; (3) error codes unchanged for existing failures (snapshot test enumerating known codes); (4) MCP error parity test; (5) errors.py has 100% branch coverage.

TESTING: unit tests per subclass; envelope shape contract test; migration tests asserting legacy+new key coexistence; full discover green.

OBSERVABILITY: envelopes are the observability; add error-code counters to evidence packet (count by code in recent runtime.log) as a stretch noted for the evidence surface.

MAINTAINER NOTES: The hierarchy is deliberately shallow (5 subclasses). Resist per-feature subclasses; new distinctions belong in `code`, not the type tree.
close_reason: errors.py SkillboxError(RuntimeError)+5 shallow subclasses; to_payload renders back-compat envelope (new error.code/context/next_actions + legacy error_code/error.type/recoverable + deprecation marker); codes unchanged (32-code snapshot); doctor/sync/up/down raisers typed; cli INTERNAL+--verbose gating; MCP parity automatic; errors.py 100% branch cov; 248 tests green

title: K3: typed HostPath/BoxPath translation seam from persistence bindings
description:
OBJECTIVE: Introduce typed path wrappers (HostPath / BoxPath) with a single translation seam, eliminating the K3 bug class where host-side code reads in-box path strings (or vice versa) and silently touches the wrong filesystem location.

BACKGROUND: MODES K3 (0.92 confidence): path translation between the host view (./.skillbox-state/...) and the in-box view (/workspace/..., /home/sandbox/..., /monoserver/...) is an untyped convention spread across runtime_ops.py (e.g. the ingress-sync translation around lines 1486-1497), workflows, compose generation, and context rendering. The class has produced real bugs patched at symptom level (commit 0cf12fe). The mount map already exists declaratively in workspace/persistence.yaml bindings — the code just does not consume it as a type.

REASONING: a path that knows which side it lives on cannot be misused quietly; translation becomes a checked operation with an error when no binding covers the path, instead of a string that happens to exist on both sides meaning different things.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) New module (scripts/lib/paths.py so both scripts/ and runtime_manager can import): @dataclass(frozen=True) HostPath / BoxPath wrapping pathlib.Path, plus PathTranslator built from the persistence bindings + env roots; translator.to_box(host) / to_host(box) raise PathTranslationError(code=PATH_UNMAPPED) when outside every binding. (2) Migration is incremental: this bead converts the KNOWN K3 sites (ingress sync, focus state writes, context generation targets, compose override generation) and any site touched by the S6 atomic-writes bead; full-codebase conversion is explicitly out of scope. (3) Add a regression tripwire: a unit test enumerating the converted call sites via a small registry (or grep-based check) so reverting to raw translation in those files fails CI. (4) repr/str must keep ergonomics (logs print plain paths).

FILES: scripts/lib/paths.py (new), runtime_manager/runtime_ops.py, context_rendering.py, workflows.py (touched sites only), tests/test_paths.py (new).

RISKS: over-zealous wrapping creating ceremony — keep the API two calls deep maximum; bindings with env-var indirection (SKILLBOX_MONOSERVER_HOST_ROOT default chains) must resolve identically to compose semantics (reuse the existing resolution helpers, do not duplicate).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) translator round-trips every binding declared in persistence.yaml; (2) unmapped path -> PATH_UNMAPPED error, not a guess; (3) the K3 incident scenario (host-side read of an in-box ingress path) reproduced as a test and fails loudly; (4) converted sites listed in the module docstring; (5) zero behavior change for valid paths (goldens stable).

TESTING: property-style round-trip tests across all bindings; negative tests (unmapped, relative, symlink escape); regression test for the original 0cf12fe scenario.

OBSERVABILITY: PATH_UNMAPPED errors carry the offending path + the binding table summary in context.

MAINTAINER NOTES: The win condition is not "everything wrapped" — it is "the seam exists, the known-burned sites use it, and new translation code has an obviously correct tool to reach for".
close_reason: HostPath/BoxPath typed seam with PathTranslator from persistence.yaml bindings. K3 sites in runtime_ops/context_rendering/workflows converted. 4 tests pass, ruff clean.

title: K5: discriminated typed schema for parity-ledger rows
description:
OBJECTIVE: Give parity-ledger rows a discriminated, validated schema (typed per surface_type) so malformed or semantically inconsistent ledger entries fail at load with precise errors instead of being coerced case-by-case downstream. MODES K5 root fix.

BACKGROUND: parity_ledger rows are untyped dicts with fields legacy_surface, surface_type (service|env_target|helper|flag), action (declare|bridge|build|drop), ownership_state (covered|bridge-only|deferred|external), intended_profiles, bridge_dependency, request_error, notes. Different surface_types make different fields meaningful (a `flag` has no bridge_dependency; a bridge-only row REQUIRES one). MODES K5 (0.88) traced repeated coercion patches (e.g. commit 6c8cc84) to this looseness. The ledger is enforced at request time (LOCAL_RUNTIME_SERVICE_DEFERRED etc.), so wrong rows mean wrong runtime refusals.

REASONING: the ledger is a CONTRACT between overlay authors and the runtime; contracts deserve schemas. Discriminating on surface_type turns "field soup" into four small well-understood shapes.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) stdlib dataclasses in scripts/lib/runtime_model.py (or a sibling parity_schema.py): ServiceSurfaceRow / EnvTargetRow / HelperRow / FlagRow with a parse_ledger_row(dict) factory dispatching on surface_type; cross-field rules encoded per class (bridge-only => bridge_dependency required; deferred => request_error required and must be a known LOCAL_RUNTIME_* code; covered => intended_profiles non-empty). (2) Validation runs during build_runtime_model; failures raise the typed ValidationError (from the error-hierarchy bead if landed; else LocalRuntimeContractError) with code PARITY_LEDGER_INVALID and row provenance (client, index, file). (3) Back-compat: one release of warn-not-fail for rows that only violate NEW cross-field rules (not structurally broken), controlled by a strictness flag defaulting to warn, flipped to fail in a follow-up noted in the bead trail. (4) Downstream consumers (validation.py ledger enforcement, parity_report.py) switch from dict.get chains to typed attributes — delete the coercion shims as you go.

FILES: scripts/lib/runtime_model.py or parity_schema.py (new), validation.py, parity_report.py, tests/test_local_runtime.py extensions.

RISKS: existing operator overlays in the wild with sloppy rows — the warn release exists precisely for them; parity_report output shape must stay stable (it has consumers in stewardship-report).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) every surface_type has a typed row class with documented required/forbidden fields; (2) a bridge-only row without bridge_dependency fails (or warns in release 1) naming client+row; (3) parity_report runs unchanged on valid ledgers (golden/diff check); (4) coercion shim code removed from the two consumer modules; (5) overlay-authoring docs updated with the per-type field tables.

TESTING: table-driven parse tests (valid + each violation per type); round-trip test (typed -> payload dict -> identical report output); regression on the 6c8cc84 scenario.

OBSERVABILITY: validation failures carry row provenance; warn-mode emits one summarized warning per client, not per row spam.

MAINTAINER NOTES: stdlib only — the MODES suggestion of pydantic is intentionally not followed (repo dependency posture). The factory + dataclasses pattern is the house style for future manifest schemas too.
close_reason: Discriminated parity-ledger schema with 4 surface types, cross-field validation, warn/strict modes, 14 tests pass. Report shape preserved.

title: Slug-validate all runtime IDs before filesystem use
description:
OBJECTIVE: Validate service/task/client/profile IDs as strict slugs before any code joins them into filesystem paths, closing the path-shape/traversal footgun MODES A8 §F4 identified.

BACKGROUND: IDs from runtime.yaml and overlays flow into paths like logs/<service-id>/, .skillbox-state/<client>/..., pid files, and lock names. An ID like "a/b" changes directory shape; "../x" escapes the intended root. Today nothing rejects these at model load; A8 rated it high-confidence as an un-tripped landmine (operator-authored YAML, so exploitation requires operator error — but agents author overlays too via client-init/blueprints).

REASONING: one regex at load time removes an entire what-if from every downstream join, forever; the brain's graph_node_id scheme and the dryrun marker filenames also benefit from guaranteed-safe IDs.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) Canonical pattern ^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,63}$ (confirm against EVERY existing in-tree ID first — run an audit script over runtime.yaml, overlays, box-profiles, boxes.json and list violations in the PR; if legitimate IDs violate (dots?), widen deliberately and document). (2) Enforce in build_runtime_model for services/tasks/clients/profiles/repos/artifacts/skills/checks; error code RUNTIME_ID_INVALID with the offending ID, its kind, and source file. (3) box.py already validates box ids/profiles (_validate_box_id etc.) — align patterns so one documented grammar covers both sides; the scripts/lib consolidation bead will physically share the code, this bead only aligns the RULES and adds the model-load enforcement. (4) client-init/onboard scaffolding rejects bad slugs at creation time with the same message.

FILES: scripts/lib/runtime_model.py, runtime_manager/validation.py, workflows.py (client-init), tests.

RISKS: an existing operator overlay with a non-conforming ID bricking render — the audit-first step is mandatory; provide a clear rename playbook in the error's next_actions (which files to update together: overlay.yaml, compose overrides, parity ledger, focus state).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) `a/b`, `../x`, leading dash, empty, 200-char IDs all rejected at render/doctor with RUNTIME_ID_INVALID + provenance; (2) all in-tree manifests pass untouched (or are renamed within this bead); (3) client-init refuses bad slugs; (4) grammar documented once in docs and referenced from both runtime_model.py and box.py.

TESTING: table-driven accept/reject tests; integration test that a planted bad service ID fails doctor; client-init rejection test.

OBSERVABILITY: error includes kind + file provenance.

MAINTAINER NOTES: Resist "sanitize" (silently rewriting IDs) — reject loudly. Sanitization would desync IDs across the model, the graph, and on-disk layout.
close_reason: Slug grammar ^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,63}$ enforced in build_runtime_model for all 8 id kinds; audit found 0 violations (no real id broken); leaf contract kept (RuntimeIdValidationError promoted to typed ValidationError/RUNTIME_ID_INVALID at cli boundary with provenance); client-init rejects bad slugs; grammar documented once; render+doctor exit 0; 245-test broad verify green

title: Model-load cycle + dangling-ref detection for depends_on graphs
description:
OBJECTIVE: Detect service/task depends_on cycles at model-validation time (render/doctor) instead of only when the agent-ops graph is built, so a cyclic overlay fails fast with the cycle path spelled out.

BACKGROUND: agent_graph_algorithms.py already implements cycle_evidence/SCC, but it runs only via brain.graph. The runtime lifecycle (order_service_ids / service_dependency_graph in runtime_ops.py) and validation.py do their own ordering; a cycle authored in an overlay surfaces late (during up, as a confusing ordering failure) or only if someone runs the brain. The 2026-06-12 architecture audit flagged this gap explicitly.

REASONING: declarative graphs are the product's spine (Design Stance #4); a declared graph that cannot be ordered is a validation error by definition and should fail at declaration-checking time with the same quality as every other doctor finding.

IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS: (1) In validate_runtime_model (validation.py), build the service depends_on digraph and the task digraph (plus service->bootstrap_task edges) for the ACTIVE scope and run cycle detection — reuse agent_graph_algorithms.cycle_evidence via a thin import (it is dependency-free) rather than re-implementing; if import layering forbids it (scripts/lib vs runtime_manager direction), move the pure algorithm into a shared leaf module both import. (2) Error code LOCAL_RUNTIME_DEPENDENCY_CYCLE with the cycle rendered as a -> b -> c -> a and each edge's source file. (3) Also validate dangling depends_on references (service depends on undeclared id) with LOCAL_RUNTIME_DEPENDENCY_UNKNOWN if not already covered — audit first, it may exist. (4) Doctor surfaces both as failing checks; up/down refuse to start with the same error rather than a generic ordering failure.

FILES: runtime_manager/validation.py, agent_graph_algorithms.py (or new shared leaf module), runtime_ops.py (error propagation), tests.

RISKS: import-direction tangles between scripts/lib and runtime_manager — the shared-leaf-module fallback is the documented escape; profile-scoped graphs (a cycle may only exist when two profiles co-activate) — validate the active scope and add an advisory all-profiles pass in doctor.

ACCEPTANCE: (1) fixture overlay with svc A->B->A fails render+doctor with the cycle path; (2) cross-profile cycle reported by the doctor advisory pass; (3) dangling depends_on covered; (4) clean manifests unaffected; (5) `up` on a cyclic model returns the typed error, never a partial start.

TESTING: unit fixtures for 2-cycles, 3-cycles, self-loops, task-vs-service mixed cycles, dangling refs; regression: existing ordering tests green.

OBSERVABILITY: cycle error includes full path + provenance per edge.

MAINTAINER NOTES: One algorithm, two consumers — if you find yourself writing a second DFS, stop and extract the leaf module instead.
close_reason: Cycle + dangling-ref detection added to validation.py with graph_cycle_evidence leaf module; tests pass; lifecycle refuses bad graphs

title: Validate box_id in box.py CLI argparse (parity with MCP _validate_identifier)
description: scripts/box.py accepts box_id as a free-form argparse string with no type= validator (cmd_up/cmd_down/etc; contrast --host/--ssh-user which use _validate_host/_validate_ssh_user). The MCP server validates box_id via _validate_identifier, but box.py is a documented first-class entrypoint and the guard/install paths call it directly. Not a command-injection bug (shlex.join neutralizes argv), but: (a) the CLI is a less-guarded trust boundary than the MCP tool that wraps it; (b) the destructive-op guard builds the dry-run marker path from the raw box_id while the MCP builds it via a validated slug, so a box_id accepted by one and rejected by the other can never line up the gate marker. Fix: add type=_validate_box_id (slug validator mirroring _IDENTIFIER_RE) to every box_id argparse argument in box.py build_parser. Existing box ids (jeremy, spaps-website, portfolio-devbox) are already valid slugs.
close_reason: box.py now validates every box_id positional at argparse boundary; focused/full box tests passed
